question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) any correspondence they have received from His Majesty's Government regarding the Windsor Framework Tariff Duty Reimbursement Scheme; (ii) the anticipated timeframe for its introduction; and (iii) any progress made on the one-stop-shop on regulatory divergence referenced in the agreed report on improvements to the Windsor Framework by Lord Murphy.

To ask the Minister of Finance whether (i) the Civil Service HR Strategy will apply to all Departments across the Civil Service; and (ii) any consideration has been given to including the 101 arm's-length bodies in the strategy, as these account for approximately 75 per cent of the overall NI Executive public pay budget.
answer_text: The NICS People Strategy 2025-2030 provides the overarching framework for Civil Service workforce renewal. The People Strategy applies to all departments across the Civil Service. The People Strategy does not extend to arm’slength bodies (ALBs). ALBs operate under separate governance and management arrangements agreed with their sponsoring Departments.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) any procedures he has put in place to replace the veterinary medicines that where stockpiled prior to the implementation of new EU regulations in January 2026; and (ii) whether any assessment has been made on why some alternatives to usual pet medication which is not available to consumers in Northern Ireland after January 2026 can cost twice as much.
answer_text: In line with the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, the supply of veterinary medicines, including restocking and any assessment of their availability in Northern Ireland, falls under the direction and control of the Secretary of State for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs (Defra). Furthermore, the Veterinary Medicines Directorate (an Executive Agency of Defra) is the Competent Authority for veterinary medicines in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in light of her Written Ministerial Statement on 18 March 2026, to detail (i) any changes she anticipates to the current system of voluntary developer contributions for wastewater infrastructure; and (ii) how her announcement will, in any way, change how Northern Ireland Water will close the investment gap they are facing.
answer_text: Under current legislation, NI Water cannot accept developer contributions and developers can only install their own infrastructure to enable connections. The voluntary developer scheme I have announced would allow developers, if they wish to do so, to contribute directly to upgrading NI Water wastewater assets where capacity constraints are preventing new connections.While this will not close NI Water’s investment gap, it can help narrow it by offsetting the costs of relieving wastewater constraints.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) any economic analysis his Department conducted on the impact of a 6 per cent rise in shipping in relation to the Greenhouse Gas Emissions Trading Scheme (Amendment) (Extension to Maritime Activities) Order 2026; and (ii) whether he will publish minutes of any discussions he has had with (a) the Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs; and (b) the devolved governments, on this issue and place them in the Assembly Library.
answer_text: A full impact assessment, which considered impacts on the whole of the UK, including on NI, was published alongside the UK ETS Authority’s main response to the “UK ETS Scope Expansion: Maritime Sector” consultation in November 2025. The UK Government, through the Department for Energy Security and Net Zero and the Department for Transport, led on the development of this assessment in conjunction with input from my department, and the governments of Scotland and Wales. This analysis shows that the economic impact of this policy is expected to be minor. At my department’s request, the UK ETS Authority also commissioned additional analysis during the policy development process to understand potential impacts on three specific NI to GB routes. This analysis, undertaken by Frontier Economics, is referenced in Annex D of the main impact assessment. Both reports have been published: The full impact assessment: https://assets.publishing.service.gov.uk/media/692591b59fd433badebc3140/uk-ets-domestic-maritime-authority-response-ia.pdf Additional Analysis Report - https://www.frontier-economics.com/media/5hmhnehy/the-impacts-of-the-uk-domestic-maritime-sector-joining-the-uk-ets.pdf	The analysis does not assess the impact of a 6 percent rise in shipping costs specifically. The impact assessment published by the UK ETS Authority incorporates economic analysis, carbon market impacts and an assessment of the potential impact on UK consumers. In addition, the report also reflects on the impacts in the EU and notes that surcharges as a result of EU ETS expansion to the maritime sector typically fall within the range of 1 – 5% of container freight rates reinforcing the expectation that the UK ETS expansion is unlikely to result in significant consumer price increases. 		Given transport costs represent only a small proportion of the final costs of goods, the impact of the UK ETS on the overall cost of producing and transporting a good for businesses is expected to be minimal.With regard to discussions with UK government and the devolved administrations, the documents to which I have provided links were developed collaboratively between my department and the UK, Scottish and Welsh governments to inform the policy positions detailed in The Greenhouse Gas Emissions Trading Scheme (Amendment) (Extension to Maritime Activities) Order 2026, and are publicly available
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the planned investment for additional elective care in 2026/27.
answer_text: Whilst the 2026/2027 Budget is currently in draft, given the scale of waiting lists and the need to plan elective care activity on a sustainable basis, I have asked my officials and the wider Health & Social Care system to progress work in line with the priorities set out in the Elective Care Framework Funding and Implementation Plan. This plan sets out the activities and investment required to tackle our waiting lists on a sustainable basis. This includes a combination of in-house capacity, regional solutions and, where appropriate, independent sector provision to maximise activity and reduce waiting times.  This is coupled with a strong efficiency agenda to drive productivity in the system.I would like to again highlight the progress we have achieved through the Elective Care Framework Implementation and Funding Plan so far this year. This includes over 211,000 additional time critical or red-flag procedures delivered since April, a reduction pf 79% in patients waiting over four years in named procedures, and the increasing throughput of elective services through primary care. The recently published statistics showing reductions in first outpatient, inpatient and diagnostic waiting lists are testament to these efforts, but much more needs to be done.Tackling our waiting list is one of my priorities and indeed is an Executive priority. Further detail on plans for expenditure in 2026/27 on waiting lists will be provided once the Executive has finalised the Budget position however in the meantime I want to assure to you that the HSC system remains focused on building on the achievements of 25/26. Sustained, multi-year investment is vital to ensure that we can maintain this momentum and it is critical that the Executive confirms support for and agreement on the draft budget allocation to address this agreed priority, as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any economic impact assessment his Department has made into the maritime Emissions Trading Scheme regulations that are coming into effect from July 2026.
answer_text: In relation to the Greenhouse Gas Emissions Trading Scheme (Amendment) (Expansion to Maritime Activities Order) 2026, a full impact assessment, which considered impacts on the whole of the UK, including NI, was published alongside the main response to the “UK ETS Scope Expansion: Maritime Sector” consultation in November 2025. This analysis shows that the impact is expected to be minor.This assessment can be accessed at the following link:https://assets.publishing.service.gov.uk/media/692591b59fd433badebc3140/uk-ets-domestic-maritime-authority-response-ia.pdfAt my department’s request, the UK ETS Authority also undertook additional analysis during the policy development process to understand potential impacts on three specific NI routes. This analysis concluded that there was a low risk of carbon leakage or modal shift across these routes. It also concluded that a carbon price significantly higher than is currently envisaged for the UK ETS would be required for there to be a material impact on maritime demand. In addition, the risk of competitive disadvantage was assessed to be low. This is due to the parallel expansion of the EU ETS to international maritime, which reduces cost differentials between competing journeys.		The outcome of the impact assessment is supported by the analysis that has been completed on the EU scheme which is already in place, and which also indicates that there should be minimal impact. 		Given transport costs represent only a small proportion of the final costs of goods, the impact of the UK ETS on the overall cost of producing and transporting a good for businesses is expected to be minimal.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) any safeguards attached to funding to higher and further education institutions; (ii) whether the adoption of the International Holocaust Remembrance Alliance (IHRA) definition of antisemitism is required to be adopted from higher and further education institutions, before funding is transferred; and (iii) whether she will make the adoption of the IHRA definition of antisemitism to be made compulsory for all higher and further education institutions, in receipt of Executive funding.
To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department has any plans for Straid Primary School, which closed at the end of the 2023-2024 academic year.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has confirmed the former Straid Primary School site has been declared surplus to requirements. The EA is progressing the disposal of the lands in line with the Land and Property Services guidance for the disposal of surplus public sector property. question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the information that he used to agree to 50 per cent derogation of carbon tax in accordance with the Maritime (ETS) regulations; (ii) the information he used to not follow the Scottish Government to seek a 100 per cent derogation for its shipping to its islands and peninsulas; and (iii) whether his officials had examined derogations applied in the Mediterranean and EU peripheries, on which the UK SI Maritime (ETS) regulations have been based, before making recommendations to him, and to publish same.
answer_text: In relation to the Greenhouse Gas Emissions Trading Scheme (Amendment) (Expansion to Maritime Activities Order) 2026:i) The 50% exemption which I have secured will help mitigate the impact on NI consumers. This has been welcomed by stakeholders. This represents a significant win for NI and ensures parity in carbon pricing obligations across the Irish Sea, as voyages between the Republic of Ireland and GB already face 50% emissions coverage, and have done since the inclusion of the maritime sector in the EU Scheme since 2024. 91% of respondents to the “UK ETS Scope Expansion: Maritime Sector” consultation agreed that routes between Northern Ireland and Great Britain should face equivalent carbon pricing options to those from the Republic of Ireland. ii) The reason for the 100% exemption for Scottish islands is due to the Scottish Government’s legal duties under the Islands (Scotland) Act 2018, to consider the impacts on and vulnerability of the small island populations and communities, and their reliance on these routes for lifeline services like education and emergency healthcare. This exemption also considers the risk of further depopulation. Routes between NI and GB would not be considered lifeline in that respect.  Should the threshold for inclusion in scope be reduced in the future, I would ensure that any potentially impacted routes in NI, such as the Rathlin ferry, would be highlighted as similar to the Scottish islands case, and if necessary, I would robustly seek a similar exemption.iii) The EU ETS, by way of amending Directive 2023/959, provides for a Member State to request a temporary derogation for certain maritime transport activities with islands with a population of fewer than 200,000 permanent residents, for example Ibiza or Menorca. NI, with a current population of ten times that figure, at almost 2 million, is not directly comparable so such an exemption was not considered.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

To ask the Minister of Finance, in light of the Northern Ireland Fiscal Council's report on The Finance Minister's proposed 2026-2027 to 2028-29/29-30 Budget, to detail the extent to which the allocation of capital spending is locked into existing projects, regardless of whether these projects are assessed to be value for money or not.
answer_text: Departmental Capital DEL budgets are developed from a zero-baseline informed by information provided by departments.While the informationgathering exercise that supported my draft budget proposals asked departments to categorise their capital bids as inescapable, precommitted, high priority, or desirable, the majority of capital funding—other than allocations specifically earmarked by the Treasury or the Executive—is issued to departments as a general allocation.It is therefore for each Executive Minister to determine how best to utilise these general capital allocations in line with their own priorities, taking account of their contractual and policy commitments within existing project pipelines.A project’s valueformoney assessment is carried out at the business case stage before any expenditure is committed.  It is the responsibility of each department to ensure that their spending proposals deliver value for money.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department will urgently update flood mapping and modelling, given that it is currently based on 2008 data.
answer_text: My Department’s Rivers Directorate has developed flood hazard mapping for all river, coastal and surface water sources using predictive computer-modelling techniques. These modelling methods are widely used by flood management authorities across these islands, ensuring alignment with recognised best practice.Flood maps were first published by my Department in November 2008. This was high level strategic mapping to indicate general areas that may be at risk of flooding. In June 2014 my Department published a suite of more detailed flood mapping to provide greater confidence and granularity of fluvial and tidal flood risk. Since then, my Department has published almost 90 map updates. The most recent update was for the Antrim town area and this was published on 5 February 2026. In general, flood maps and models are reviewed on a six-year rolling cycle and updated as necessary to maintain the most accurate representation of flood risk. In addition, periodic updates following any significant change for example, after the construction of a new flood alleviation scheme, are also undertaken.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any processes they are putting in place to allow external auditors to undertake performance management and monitoring of senior Civil Service staff.
answer_text: The Civil Service Performance Management policy applies to all staff, including the Senior Civil Service (‘SCS’). The policy is currently under review, including consideration of implementing separate performance management policy arrangements for the SCS.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, in light of the Northern Ireland Fiscal Council's report on The Finance Minister's proposed 2026-2027 to 2028-29/29-30 Budget, for his assessment of the observation that rates based spending power has not increased significantly in real terms, and shows that there is no marked fiscal effort being built into future budget plans.
answer_text: Many households continue to struggle with the cost of living and businesses are experiencing higher costs of doing business. Therefore, when setting the Regional Rate, the Executive must strike a difficult balance between generating income and not pushing families and businesses into hardship. I believe my proposals strike the right balance with the proposed increases generating an additional £250 million over the Budget period.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on any progress that has been made on granting environmental permission for critical seabed infrastructure, particularly in relation to new electricity seabed interconnections and for offshore wind cabling.
answer_text: Under the Marine and Coastal Access Act 2009, DAERA as the appropriate authority, must ensure that essential development within the marine area can proceed cohesively, fairly and sustainably while protecting the marine environment, human health and legitimate uses of the sea.There are currently two live marine survey licences, both issued by the department in 2025 to inform the location and routing of cable corridors for an electricity interconnector project and a proposed offshore wind project.  Details of these and all other live marine licences can be found on the DAERA Marine Licensing online Public Register.The department is also supporting the environmental work being taken forward under the Offshore Renewable Energy Action Plan (OREAP) to identify suitable areas for offshore renewable energy development while minimising impacts on the marine environment.  This will inform future marine licence applications for cabling and associated infrastructure.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for an update on the status of the Reservoirs Act (Northern Ireland) 2015; (ii) to detail the number of dams that have recently had safety inspections and the number that have yet to be inspected; and (iii) whether she has full responsibility and accountability for all reservoirs.
answer_text: The Reservoirs Act (NI) 2015 (the Act) received Royal Assent in July 2015 with fifteen sections and one schedule of the Act coming into force at that time. Implementing the remainder of the Act requires further secondary legislation to be made.  The Executive agreed to the progressing of five pieces of intertwined secondary legislation which would further commence the majority of the provisions of the Act in October 2024 and the Department tabled the legislation with the Committee for Infrastructure in November 2024.Once the relevant sections of the Act relating to reservoir safety inspections are commenced, my Department will be able to monitor and report on compliance with requirements to commission reservoir inspections.The Committee scrutiny process remains ongoing at present and therefore a public safety risk remains until such times as the secondary legislation comes into force. Until the Act is fully implemented, my Department will continue to remind reservoir managers of their Common Law responsibilities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any discussions he has had with the shipping industry on the implementation of the UK Emissions Trading Scheme.
answer_text: Both I and my department have engaged extensively with the maritime sector with respect to proposed developments with the UK Emissions Trading Scheme (ETS), and this includes with key shipping industry contacts.I ensured that the potential impact on NI stakeholders was a key consideration in the formulation of policy options, and that all potentially affected stakeholders were directly issued with copies of consultation documentation and associated responses, including the “UK Emissions Trading Scheme: Scope Expansion- Maritime Sector” consultation in November 2024, and subsequent responses in July and November 2025 respectively. A number of these stakeholders provided comprehensive responses to the consultation.These stakeholders were also invited to, and participated in, a number of online consultation events during the consultation period, and my department, in conjunction with the Department for Energy Security and Net Zero (DESNZ), hosted an in-person stakeholder event in Belfast to discuss the expansion of the UK ETS into the maritime sector. NI was the only region of the UK to hold such an event, given the reliance on maritime transport for this region of the UK.Through this engagement, I have ensured that all potential impacts on NI were identified and considered across the UK in the formulation of the policy design of the planned expansion of scope of the scheme to the sector.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in light of the Metrology Office warning that potential extreme rainfall may be expected for up to another month, to detail any processes she is putting in place to ensure that the recent inter-agency flooding response, is not repeated.
answer_text: As the Lead Government Department for the strategic co-ordination of the emergency response to significant flooding my officials have worked with partner organisations, including Met Office, to develop a robust and coordinated multi-agency severe weather plan. Overall my Department’s preparations for the response to severe weather, including flooding, is not confined to particular events but is an ongoing process throughout the year with the Department operating an on-call Duty and Flooding Officer Rota system 24 hours a day, 365 days a year. Despite the proactive work of my Department and multi-agency partners, which often protect many properties from the impacts of severe weather, it is not possible to prevent all flooding. The recent heavy rainfall arrived following the third wettest Autumn on record and the second wettest January, with 100.8mm of rainfall recorded in a single day at Katesbridge, well beyond previous site records. In preparation for severe weather, my officials have continued to monitor river levels and to undertake targeted inspections and clearance of flood risk management infrastructure as necessary at locations known to be vulnerable during prolonged rainfall. These activities help ensure that drainage systems remain clear and capable of conveying flows as intended.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they have received a formal invitation to visit the President of the United States of America in March 2026.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any discussions he has had with Department for Energy Security and Net Zero on the implications of the UK Emissions Trading Scheme on GB to NI shipping routes.
answer_text: As a member of the UK Emissions Trading Scheme (ETS) Authority, both I and my department are in constant contact with other Authority members (the Department for Energy Security and Net Zero (DESNZ), and Scottish and Welsh Governments) including as part of the Net Zero, Energy and Climate Change Inter Ministerial Group. No policy decision can be taken with respect to the development of the UK ETS without my agreement, and that of other Ministers across the UK Administrations.Expansion of scope of the UK ETS to include emissions from the maritime sector was first proposed in the “Developing the UK Emissions Trading Scheme” consultation in March 2022.Since coming into office, I have ensured that potential impacts on NI were front and centre in the formulation of the policy proposals consulted on in the “UK Emissions Trading Scheme: Scope Expansion: Maritime Sector consultation”, published in November 2024, and I fully considered and approved the policy positions contained in both the interim and main responses to this consultation, published in July and November 2025 respectively. This included securing a 50% exemption for NI - GB shipping routes, to ensure parity of coverage with routes between Ireland and GB, thus ensuring that NI vessels operating on these routes were not disadvantaged. I have kept Committee and Executive colleagues updated throughout the policy development process.These policy positions are reflected in The Greenhouse Gas Emissions Trading Scheme (Amendment) (Extension to Maritime Activities) Order 2026, which was laid before the NI Assembly on the 13 January.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any discussions he has had with the logistics industry on (i) any rise in shipping costs created by the implementation of the UK Emissions Trading Scheme; and (ii) any disruption and divergence of logistics vehicles and shipping containers from Cairnryan, Heysham and Birkenhead ports.
answer_text: Both I and my department have engaged extensively with the maritime sector with respect to proposed developments with the UK Emissions Trading Scheme (ETS), and this includes with key logistics industry contacts.I ensured that the potential impact, including potential cost implications, on NI stakeholders was a key consideration in the formulation of policy options, and that all potentially affected stakeholders were directly issued with copies of consultation documentation and associated responses, including the “UK Emissions Trading Scheme: Scope Expansion- Maritime Sector” consultation in November 2024, and subsequent responses in July and November 2025 respectively. A number of these stakeholders provided comprehensive responses to the consultation.Stakeholders were also invited to, and participated in, a number of online consultation events during the consultation period, and my department, in conjunction with the Department for Energy Security and Net Zero (DESNZ), hosted an in-person stakeholder event in Belfast to discuss the expansion of the UK ETS into the maritime sector. NI was the only region of the UK to hold such an event, given the reliance on maritime transport for this region of the UK.As part of the assessment of impacts undertaken alongside the policy development process, the UK ETS Authority also commissioned a report to look specifically at the impacts on three NI-GB Routes in particular. The routes selected included Heysham-Warrenpoint in respect of roll on, roll-off cargo. This report concluded that there was a low risk of carbon leakage or modal shift across the routes analysed. It also concluded that a carbon price significantly higher than is currently envisaged for the UK ETS would be required for there to be a material impact on maritime demand. In addition, the risk of competitive disadvantage was assessed to be low. This is due to the parallel expansion of the EU ETS to international maritime, which reduces cost differentials between competing journeys.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, as we approach 250th anniversary of the US Declaration of Independence, whether he has asked representatives from the US Armed Forces to visit Northern Ireland, to highlight our combined roles in the defence of the Atlantic over the last eight decades.
answer_text: I have not yet formally asked representatives from the US Armed Forces to visit Northern Ireland as part of the 250th anniversary of the US Declaration of Independence.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline the implications of EU Carbon Trading regulations on energy costs in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The EU’s carbon trading rules do not create direct additional costs for households or businesses here.Responsibility for the Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism, or CBAM, sits primarily with DAERA. My officials have confirmed that we are not liable for EU CBAM charges in our wholesale electricity costs as we operate through the Windsor Framework as part of the internal EU Electricity. In addition, electricity arriving through the Moyle Interconnector is not subject to any carbon border cost.In December, the UK and EU agreed to explore the re-entry of the UK into the EU Internal Electricity market and when this happens, there will obviously no longer be CBAM issues between Britain and the EU.Furthermore, there is a commitment to consider the alignment of the UK and EU’s respective Emissions Trading Systems as part of the UK/EU reset – this alignment would further protect our consumers from unnecessary costs that might emerge from carbon price divergence.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the status of the Reservoirs Act (Northern Ireland) 2015, including whether all provisions have been fully implemented.
answer_text: The Reservoirs Act (Northern Ireland) 2015 (the Act) received Royal Assent in July 2015 with fifteen sections and one schedule of the Act coming into force at that time.  Implementing the remainder of the Act requires further secondary legislation to be made.The Executive agreed to the progressing of five pieces of intertwined secondary legislation which would further commence the majority of the provisions of the Act in October 2024 and the Department tabled the legislation with the Committee for Infrastructure in November 2024.The Committee scrutiny process remains ongoing at present and therefore a public safety risk remains until such times as the secondary legislation comes into force.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) when the last full audit of the Reservoirs Act (Northern Ireland) 2015 was conducted; (ii) the number of reservoirs that were designated as being in poor or very poor conditions; and (iii) whether she has full responsibility and accountability of all aspects of Reservoirs Act (Northern Ireland) 2015.
answer_text: In 2016, at the request of the Agriculture and Rural Development Committee, a reservoirs audit to identify the condition of potential controlled reservoirs in the North was undertaken.  This voluntary audit revealed that, of the 105 reservoirs for which data was obtained, at that time 45 were deemed to be in either Poor or Very Poor condition.   The Department undertook further analysis of the 45 reservoirs in 2019, completing this work in 2020 and formally shared the findings including any potential risks with the relevant reservoir managers. My Department is responsible for bringing forward the secondary legislation to further commence the Reservoirs Act (NI) 2015, which is currently subject to Assembly scrutiny procedures.  Once fully commenced the Act, will introduce a regulatory framework for reservoir safety and will place duties on Reservoir Managers for the safety of their reservoir. Until the Act is fully implemented, my Department encourages all reservoir managers to adopt the best practice principles which underpin the legislation and engages with them to remind them of their Common Law responsibilities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when the Northern Ireland Ordnance Survey will update its historical flooding overlays in light of the recent floods.
answer_text: Responsibility for the development, maintenance, and publication of flood mapping rests solely with my Department. These are developed and updated in line with industry standards Aerial photography, captured through drone footage or similar means, is required to update the historical flooding layer on Flood Maps NI. This information is often challenging to gather during flood events due to health and safety constraints, the geographical scale of flooding, and more frequently the adverse weather conditions that prevent the safe use of drones. Therefore, as there was only a very limited window to undertake this work on some occasions, and this was the case for the Sixmilewater river during Storm Chandra, it was not possible to capture the flooding extents. Following on from the recent Sixmilewater Adjournment Debate, during which you raised a range of wider questions on flood mapping and flood risk management, I have asked my Officials to engage with your office on any flood mapping concerns you may have and more fully explain my Department’s  flood mapping, data, and emerging technologies which are used effectively to manage flood risk across the North.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of the implications on (i) cost; (ii) security; and (iii) continuity of electricity supply in Northern Ireland, as a result of EU Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism directives, that came into force 1 Jan 2026.
answer_text: Responsibility for the Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism, or CBAM, sits primarily with DAERA. My officials have confirmed that we are not liable for EU CBAM charges in our wholesale electricity costs as we operate through the Windsor Framework as part of the internal EU Electricity market. In addition, electricity arriving through the Moyle Interconnector is not subject to any carbon border cost. Therefore, there are no direct cost, security or continuity implications for our electricity supply.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) any request received from the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Development or the Department for Infrastructure, for updates of flooding maps provided by Ordnance Survey of Northern Ireland (OSNI); and (ii) the last time the OSNI flooding modelling was updated.
answer_text: Responsibility for the development, maintenance, and publication of flood mapping rests solely with my Department. Flood hazard mapping for all river, coastal and surface water sources have been developed using predictive computer-modelling techniques. These modelling methods are widely used by flood management authorities across these islands, ensuring alignment with recognised best practice.Flood maps and models are reviewed on a six-year rolling cycle and updated as necessary to maintain the most accurate representation of flood risk. The flood hazard mapping are published on the Flood Maps NI web viewer and shared with our business partners, Land and Property Services Ordnance Survey of Northern Ireland, who commercially license the data as digital products.The most recent update to the flood mapping was for the Antrim town area and this was completed on 5 February 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, in view of the recent NI Audit Office report Leading and Resourcing the Northern Ireland Civil Service, (i) to detail any criteria by which the Head of the Civil Service could have been assessed as satisfactory under NICS HR guidance, resulting in a renumeration package increase of £30,000 to £290,000; and (ii) whether he will place in the NI Assembly library the NICS HR criteria for senior salary pay awards and review.
answer_text: The Civil Service Performance Management policy applies to all staff, including the Senior Civil Service (SCS), but it does not prescribe the practical application of the policy to the HOCS role.  The HOCS position is unique: the role has no line-management structure or reporting/countersigning officer to undertake the standard appraisal process provided for under the policy. Accordingly, the extant SCS performance process has not been applied to the HOCS role. The Performance Management policy is currently under review and part of that review will include consideration of performance management arrangements for the HOCS role, while safeguarding the political impartiality of the Civil Service.The eligibility criteria for each pay award are published on the Department of Finance website.  These criteria apply consistently across the nonindustrial workforce, including the Senior Civil Service.As the relevant criteria are already publicly accessible, there is no requirement to place additional copies in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in light of the increased amount of flooding expected on already saturated land, for an update of the Living With Water Programme.
answer_text: The formal programme structures for the 12-year Living With Water in Belfast Plan were stood down in December 2024, following an affordability review, prompted by a £700m increase in the estimated cost of delivering the NI Water elements of the Plan. The review reaffirmed the need for the Belfast Plan, but concluded that the original 12-year delivery timeframe is no longer achievable.  	Projects within the Belfast Plan will still proceed, led by my Department and NI Water, but at a scale and pace aligned with available budgets, rather than through a formal programme structure. 	NI Water has therefore deferred delivery of major upgrades needed to wastewater infrastructure around Belfast Lough including Sydenham Pumping Station and wastewater treatment works at Belfast, Whitehouse and Kinnegar. NI Water will instead prioritise additional maintenance to ensure these assets remain operational until funding for significant upgrades becomes available.	While the Plan was always expected to require additional investment, as referenced in the New Decade New Approach agreement, this has not been provided by the British Government.  	I remain committed to the objectives of the Belfast Plan, to help protect against flooding, enhance the environment and grow the economy, and I will continue to advocate for the additional funding needed to expedite the delivery of projects within the Plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the (i) average cost to repair a pothole in Northern Ireland; (ii) total cost of damage claims to her Department from motorists hitting potholes; and (iii) amount of potholes her Department can repair with any of the £16.8 million Capital DEL underspend she has been further allocated with.
answer_text: The management information my Department holds in relation to roads related public liability claims can be found online at: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/road-asset-maintenance-directorate-management-information-roads-related-claims-period-20202021-20242025.Table 7.1 provides details of claim expenditure for the North for the last five full financial years broken down by claim type and expenditure type. Claim figures have been extracted from DfI's Claims Management System, whereas the financial data has been extracted from the NICS accounting system, Account NI. Unfortunately, as the NICS Accounting System does not record the cause of incidents that lead to compensation payments, it is not possible to disaggregate the amount of compensation paid out by defect type.My Department does not hold information on the average cost of repairing a pothole as the cost for repairing potholes falls within the Department’s budget for Patching. This term is applied to small scale works necessary to keep roads in a satisfactory state of repair, including infilling potholes but also the repair of small areas affected by cracking or settlement.As the materials, specifications, quantities and methods used vary from location to location there is no single method or technique used in the repair of surface defects and as such it is not possible to provide a single unit cost for repairs carried out by either our internal or external contractors.For the same reason, it is not possible to determine how many potholes the recently announced additional funding of £7.85m will address, but the funding will be used on a priority basis, targeting the most severe potholes causing the highest safety concerns for the Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) any plans her Department has for repairing the Ballycorr Road, Ballyclare; and (ii) whether she will undertake a visit to the road with residents and elected representatives to listen to concerns of its condition.
answer_text: Officials have advised that overlay patching works at a rural section of the road are programmed for this financial year.Additional resurfacing works on a section of the Ballycorr Road, from its junction with Ballyeaston Road to the junction at Craighill Manor are also planned for completion within the next financial year.Details of resurfacing schemes will be included in my Department’s Annual Report to Council.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any capital funding her Department plans to allocate for the construction of the A5 Road in (i) 2026-27; (ii) 2027-28; and (iii) 2028-29.
answer_text: The delivery programme and costs for the A5 Western Transport Corridor can only be determined with any degree of certainty once the outcome of the appeal process is known.The Draft Budget 2026-29/30 has been issued by the Finance Minister for public consultation, and it is for the Executive to agree a Final Budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, in light of the proposal for a directive of the European Parliament and of the Council, amending Directives 2009/43/EC and 2009/81/EC, as regards the simplification of intra-EU transfers of defence related products and the simplification of security and defence procurement, to detail how her Written Ministerial Statement on the Invest NI Review of Investments on 16 October 2025 is compatible with the Northern Ireland Protocol and the Windsor Framework.
answer_text: My Written Ministerial Statement of 16 October 2025 outlines additional measures to ensure that public funds are not being used to support the manufacture of arms or components that are used for genocide.  Those measures do not alter or obstruct the application of EU goods rules made applicable under the Windsor Framework.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she will place in the Assembly library, all correspondence, including (i) emails; (ii) WhatsApp messages; (iii) minutes of conversations and meetings; and (iv) call logs, between the BBC and her Department, pertaining to her appearance on the Good Morning Ulster Show, on the 27 January 2026.
answer_text: The correspondence will be placed in the Assembly Library once the appropriate checks have been done to ensure the Department complies with its obligations under the UK General Data Protection Regulation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any discussions her Department has had with (i) the EU; (ii) His Majesty’s Government; and (iii) the Utility Regulator of Northern Ireland, regarding the implications for Northern Ireland's energy market from the EU regulations (a) ST 10871 2021 INIT; and (b) ST 10875 2021 INIT, bearing in mind the concerns raised by the House of Lords Northern Ireland Scrutiny Committee.
answer_text: My Department has considered the implications of EU regulatory developments for the electricity market here, within the context of wider work relating to the UK–EU Reset, the Single Electricity Market (SEM), and ongoing work relating to Emissions Trading Systems (ETS) and the Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM).Whilst CBAM is not within the official responsibilities of my Department, my officials continue to engage with the British Government and the Utility Regulator as discussions relating to UK-EU Reset, including Windsor Framework structures, progress. These engagements help ensure that our specific interests, particularly in relation to the SEM and future carbonpricing arrangements, in the context of both British and EU legislative changes, are considered.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in light of the flooding along the Six Mile River, South Antrim, and its tributaries, to detail (i) when the latest flood maps were produced of the river and its tributaries; and (ii) whether any assessment and mapping of the extent of the flooding on the 27 January 2026, has been carried out in comparison to the latest flood maps of the river and its tributaries.
answer_text: The Sixmilewater River catchment is covered by two Fluvial Hydraulic Flood Models:Sixmilewater Fluvial Model was developed in June 2016 and covers the reach from Ballynure to Dunadry. No updates to the Sixmilewater Fluvial Model are planned at this time.Antrim Fluvial Model was developed in May 2014 and covers the reach from Dunadry to the river mouth at Lough Neagh.Please note that an updated Antrim Fluvial Model has been developed and is currently undergoing quality assurance. It is expected that updated maps will be published on the Flood Maps NI web viewer by end of February 2026. My Department uses a network of hydrometric gauges across the North of Ireland to monitor river levels and flows. Data from the 27 January 2026 flood event indicates peak flow in excess of a 1 in 25-year event (approx. 4% annual exceedance probability).Modelled flood extents are available for several return periods, including the 1 in 25-year event. Initial observations made by my Department officials during the flood event indicate that   extents broadly matched both the current and draft predicted 1 in 25-year modelled extents in most areas, demonstrating the reliability of the Department’s flood modelling.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any plans she has to support (i) boiler upgrade schemes; (ii) solar panels; (iii) batteries, for low income families; and (iv) low and zero-interest loans for reducing energy bills, in line with the statement from our nation’s Energy Secretary on 20 January 2026.
answer_text: On 20 January 2026, the British Government launched the Warm Homes Plan outlining support in the areas you raise. This plan contains announcements that are focused largely on England and there is a lack of clarity as to what this means for consumers here. My officials continue to engage with DESNZ, as they bring forward the design of the schemes and initiatives contained within the plan, some of which are not due to commence until the 2027/28 financial year.  In terms of local delivery, financial assistance is available through the NISEP, for the installation of whole house retrofit measures including insulation and heat pumps, supported by solar PV and battery storage. My Department has worked to add to the NISEP budget by providing over £5m in financial support for energy efficiency in low-income households over the last 3 years. Looking forward, my Department published the Government Response to the consultation entitled Support for Low Carbon Heat in Residential Buildings Consultation on 26th January 2026. Next steps include development of a business case and regulations this year as we work to bring forward a scheme to replace NISEP. My officials continue to work with colleagues in the Department for Communities who are currently developing the Warm Healthy Homes Fund and in collaboration with the Housing Executive and industry partners across several pilot projects to identify practical solutions to reduce energy bills. These pilots will inform future retrofit options as we continue to develop policy.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any discussions they have had with (i) the Secretary of State for Northern Ireland; and (ii) HMRC, regarding the date of introduction of European Commission COM(2023) 258, and its annexes, following evidence given by HMRC at the Treasury Select Committee that the date of introduction is April 2028, rather than July 2026 as notified by the EU Commission.
answer_text: European Commission COM (2023) 258, and its annexes, is a reserved matter which is the responsibility of HMRC. The lead Department for customs-related matters, including interaction with HMRC, is the Department for the Economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether any analysis has been conducted on the economic impact of E-commerce, in light of the introduction of the European Commission's COM (2023) 258 final 2023/0156 (COD) and its Annexes, in July 2026.
answer_text: The proposed measures contained within the European Commission’s COM (2023) 258 final (2023/0156 (COD)), do not fall within the devolved responsibilities of the Department for the Economy.Matters relating to customs, VAT, excise, and the wider regulation of crossborder Ecommerce are reserved to Treasury and HMRC. As such, no analysis of the economic impact of these proposals has been undertaken by my Department.Any future application of EU measures in the North would be subject to the appropriate democratic scrutiny processes, as set out in the Windsor Framework.My Department continues to engage regularly with HMRC and other Whitehall departments to monitor developments in this area and to ensure that any implications for businesses are clearly understood and communicated.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for his assessment of the amount of the UK’s £15 billion Warm Homes Plan that will be provided in Barnett Consequential funding, in light of the Energy Secretary’s announcement on 21 January 2026
answer_text: It is yet unclear what Barnett consequentials, if any, will flow to the Executive from this announcement. Any Barnett consequentials that are received are unhypothecated, meaning that it is for the Executive to decide on their allocation in line with local priorities.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any discussions they have had with the Cabinet Office regarding the introduction of European Commission COM(2023) 258, and its annexes, in July 2026.
answer_text: European Commission COM(2023) 258, and its annexes, is a reserved matter which is the responsibility of HMRC. The lead Department for customs-related matters, including interaction with HMRC, is the Department for the Economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when the last survey of the road drainage infrastructure of the Ballyclare area, was completed.
answer_text: My Department does not routinely survey the road drainage infrastructure across our network.  We maintain a record of the network and carry out detailed surveys where there is an unresolved problem requiring further investigation.  The issues with the drainage infrastructure in Ballyclare are known to NI Water and relate primarily to their network capacity.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline any action she is taking to promote the defence and security industry in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I recognise the value of the Advanced Manufacturing and Engineering sector to the economy in the north, including the Aerospace, Defence and Space sub-sector. I am committed to supporting businesses on a cross-sectoral basis to grow, innovate and export in line with my economic vision.Invest NI works closely with businesses in this space, providing tailored advice, innovation and export support, and helping firms to access UK and international market opportunities. The Department’s Advanced Manufacturing sectoral action plan sets out how we are strengthening the capabilities that underpin competitiveness across highvalue industries, including support for innovation, skills and supply chain development.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any engagement they have had with the Cabinet Office, regarding the establishment of a one stop shop to explain the complexities of interregional trade, in accordance with the Government's acceptance of Lord Murphy’s recommendations on the Windsor Framework.

To ask the Minister of Education to detail how a school exam day was lost at Ballyclare High School due to the failure of the Education Authority to secure the use of one mechanical digger to effect repairs. [R]
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has confirmed that Ballyclare High School reported a significant mains water leak on 12 January 2026. Northern Ireland Water promptly attended and isolated the water supply to the school grounds to prevent further damage.EA contractors arrived on site the following day, 13 January 2026, to determine the exact source of the leak. Due to the extensive nature of the investigatory work and the planned excavation required, the necessary remedial works were scheduled for 14 January.The excavation and associated repair works were successfully completed on 14 January 2026, fully restoring the school's main water supply on that date.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any action her Department is taking to promote the defence and security industry, so that, as part of the Windsor Framework, Northern Ireland can comply with the proposed amending Regulations (i) (EC) No 1907/2006; (ii) (EC) No 1272/2008; (iii) (EU) No 528/2012; (iv) (EU) No 2019/1021; and (v) (EU) No 2021/697, regarding defence readiness, facilitating defence investments and conditions for the defence industry.
answer_text: Invest NI works closely with businesses in this sector, providing tailored advice, innovation and export support, and helping firms to access international market opportunities. The Department’s Advanced Manufacturing sectoral action plan sets out how we are strengthening the capabilities that underpin competitiveness across high value industries, including support for innovation, skills and supply chain development.	My Department has taken legal advice on this area for Windsor Framework engagement with the Democratic Scrutiny Committee. It is considered an Excepted Matter and for DfE areas of interest is anticipated to have no impacts.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in light of the recent fatalities following the road traffic collision on the Moira Road, Crumlin, whether her Department, with the PSNI, will conduct an urgent review of signage and road safety markings in the area.
answer_text: Firstly, I would like to express my sincere condolences to the Singco family and and all those tragically affected by the serious collision on the Moira Road, Crumlin on Sunday 11 January 2026.Every life lost on our roads is a profound loss to the community and road safety remains an extremely high priority for my Department.  We are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and address the needs of all road users.Collisions of this nature, particularly those which result in a fatality, are formally investigated by the PSNI and my officials will work with the investigating officers to facilitate their enquiries.  When findings are shared, we will carefully consider if any engineering measures can be introduced to enhance road safety at this location.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when they will answer (i) AQW 35856/22-27; and (ii) AQW 32266/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 35856/22-27 was answered on 19 January 2026.AQW 32266/22-27 was answered on 19 December 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the (i) staffing; (ii) consultant; and (iii) architect, costs associated with Kilbride Central Primary School for the (a) School Enhancement Project; and (b) Minor Works Programme, which have been significantly delayed. [R]
answer_text: School Enhancement ProgrammeThe School Enhancement Programme (SEP) project at Kilbride Central Primary School is being managed by the Education Authority (EA). An Integrated Consultancy Team (ICT) was appointed in March 2022 to take the SEP project through the design and construction phases.Since appointment, the ICT costs to date are approximately £32,000 and staffing/resource fees are approximately £53,000. These costs are comparable with other similar projects at RIBA Stage 1 (Technical Feasibility). No further design costs will be incurred until the EA receives Business Case approval to proceed to RIBA Stage 2 (Scheme Design). Minor Works ProgrammeThe EA completed a Traffic Management and Car Parking project at Kilbride Central Primary School on 30 September 2025.As of 9 January 2026, the Staffing costs are £12,425.41; Consultancy costs are £600; and the Architect fee contracted amount is £25,400. The actual spend for Architect fees as of 9 January 2026 is £22,522.50 and the remaining balance will be paid following the end of the defects period, one calendar year after completion.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) any discussions her Department has had with HM Government, around the announcement on 22 December 2025 that the European Commission and the United Kingdom should work towards the participation of the United Kingdom in the European Union’s internal electricity market, by way of a European Union-United Kingdom Electricity Agreement; and (ii) any implications for an European Union-United Kingdom Electricity Agreement, with respect to the Integrated Single Electricity Market.
answer_text: My Department’s engagement around the announcement of 22 December 2025 has been primarily with the Department for Energy Security and Net Zero (DESNZ) in London. I welcome the ongoing negotiations between Britain and the EU as part of the UK–EU relationship reset, that includes the latest announcement regarding a potential European Union–United Kingdom Electricity Agreement. The discussions remain exploratory, and any implications for the Single Electricity Market (SEM) will depend on the final form of any agreement reached. My Department remains fully committed to the continued and efficient operation of the SEM and will continue to engage with DESNZ as this process progresses.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of the safety of pupils, who now walk along pavements that are untreated for ice and are poorly lit, for up to 0.9 miles in Ballyclare, due to the recent decision by Translink and her Department's officials to reroute the school bus services. [R]
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department, and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and to address the needs of all road users. We are acutely aware that children and young people are amongst the most vulnerable groups using our roads, particularly around schools.The decision to reroute services was an operational decision from Translink in conjunction with other stakeholders. As this issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I had previously sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:Following longstanding concern, with unsuitable road and school infrastructure to permit safe ingress, boarding and egress from Ballyclare High School, Translink initiated further engagement with representatives from Ballyclare High School, the Education Authority, DfI Roads and local Policing to try an establish a safer alternative. Several options were reviewed, and the displacement of five of the vehicles/pickups to Readers Park was agreed upon. Other options, such as the use of Ballyclare Bus Depot and Ballyclare Secondary School grounds, were not suitable from the school’s perspective.   Prior to commencement of the communication and installation of the new afternoon school transport arrangements (at the beginning of November 2025), Translink conducted a risk assessment. This assessment accounted for the rerouting of the vehicles, the suitability of the road infrastructure at Readers Park, ability to allow for safe boarding and the route the children would walk (max. 700m). Upon completion of this exercise, whilst Translink acknowledge all risk has not been eliminated fully, this mitigation demonstrates a reduction in the potential occurrence and impact of possible collision with pupil, members of the public, third party vehicle and other buses. My Department gives priority to the provision of salt for the treatment of the gritted road network, after which salt is made available to local councils for the treatment of town centres and pedestrian areas.  Salt has been consistently supplied to Councils for this purpose during periods of prolonged adverse weather.RSPPG E072 details my Department’s policy for the provision of street lighting, which was established in 1993 and can be accessed via the following link.Street lighting RSPPG E072 | Department for Infrastructure (infrastructure-ni.gov.uk). This document is subject to continual review.The street lighting provision in the area serviced by the Translink rerouting of the school bus is in line with this policy and considered appropriate for the area.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the Chinese officials she met on her recent visit to China; (ii) whether her Department will publish the minutes of any meetings she had; (iii) whether an official from her Department was present at each meeting she attended; (iii) the cost of the visit, in (a) flights; (b) accommodation; (c) airport transfers; and (d) subsistence, for her and any of her officials; and (iv) whether any gifts in presents or hospitality were received and the costs therein.
answer_text: (i) I met with the following Chinese officials in four meetings during my visit to China. 1.	Chinese People's Association for Friendship with Foreign Countries (CPAFFC), BeijingVice President of Chinese People's Association for Friendship with Foreign Countries (CPAFFC)	Deputy Head of the Department of European and Central Asian Affairs 	Translator, staff of Department of European and Central Asian Affairs 	2.	Ministry of Commerce (MOFCOM), BeijingDirector General, Department of Europe, Ministry of Commerce 	Translator, staff of Department of European and Central Asian Affairs 	3.	Ministry of Education, BeijingVice Minister of the Ministry of Education Director of the Department of International Exchange and Cooperation, the Ministry of Education 	4.	Meeting with Liaoning and Shenyang officials, ShenyangVice Governor, People’s Government of Liaoning Province, Secretary Chaoyang Municipal Committee of the CPCDeputy Secretary General, People’s Government of Liaoning ProvinceForeign Affairs Office of the People’s Government of Liaoning ProvinceStanding Committee Member Shenyang Municipal Committee of the CPC – Vice Mayor, ShenyangDeputy Director, Department of Education of Liaoning ProvinceDeputy Director, Department of Commerce of Liaoning ProvinceInterpreter, Foreign Affairs Office of the People’s Government of Liaoning Province	 (ii)	Written records of meetings are retained in accordance with the Department’s policy on the retention and disposal of records as per the requirements of the Functioning of Government (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act (Northern Ireland) 2021. There is no requirement to publish minutes.	(iii) 	Yes, an official from my Department was present at each meeting I attended.			(iv) 	The cost of the visit for myself and officials;					(a) flights: including internal flights, for 4 people, totalling £10,701.05, and train costs totalling £447.60			(b) accommodation: 7 nights for 4 people, totalling £3,110.72			(c) airport transfers: no cost			(d) subsistence: for 4 people, totalling £392.14			(v) 	Officials and I received gifts to the total value of £280 and hospitality to the total value of £660.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on his Department's current financial position.
answer_text: You will be aware that Health has faced an extremely challenging year in terms of finances with a funding gap of some £615 million. I have been clear from the outset that Health could not breakeven without additional support from the Executive. While some £69 million was received to support my Ministerial Direction to implement the HSC pay award this left a residual shortfall of £140 million for pay costs alone.My department identified £300 million in savings and cost containment measures, supplemented by £25 million from June Monitoring, reducing the non-pay gap to £90 million. However, when combined with the pay shortfall, the total gap stands at £230 million. This overspend could materially impact the 2026–27 budget, as any deduction from our baseline would further constrain next year’s position.My department has been working throughout this year to deliver longer-term financial sustainability and reform of HSC services as evidenced by the Health and Social Care Reset Plan which I published in July 2025. This plan focuses on stabilisation, reform, and delivery, aiming to establish neighbourhood-centred care and drive efficiencies. While we bid for additional funding under the multi-year budget exercise to support the Reset Plan, the draft budget proposals 2026-29/30 the Finance Minister has published confirm an extremely challenging outlook for Health.Given the funding being proposed for Health, we will have generate savings internally to cover our inescapable commitments. I have advised the Finance Minister that delivering savings of this magnitude in a single year is not feasible. Therefore, we require agreement on a recovery plan over the three-year budget period. This would allow managed delivery, though it remains exceptionally challenging and not without risk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in light of the December 2025 Comptroller and Auditor General’s (C&AG) Report on Financial Audit Findings 2025 – Central Government, where his Department's accounts where qualified in respect to the discrepancy between the provision his Department made of £495,000 on the Mobuoy illegal dump, while the quantum of the provision is in the region of £107 million, to detail (i) on what grounds did his Accounting Officer make the recommendation to the treatment of the sum in the departmental accounts; (ii) on what grounds did he agre
answer_text: (i)  As part of the preparation of the NIEA and DAERA 2024-25 Annual Report and Accounts, the Departmental Finance Director and NIEA Accounting Officer undertook a careful technical assessment of the accounting treatment of the latest Mobuoy position in accordance with ‘International Accounting Standard 37 Provisions, Contingent Liabilities and Contingent Assets’ and discussed their conclusions with the DAERA Accounting Officer who supported their assessment.  It is my understanding that, having applied careful professional judgement informed by all of the analyses, contextual factors, legal obligations and wider considerations in relation to this issue including my statements on this issue in the Assembly on four occasions in 2024 and on 9 June 2025, it was determined by both Accounting Officers  that it was appropriate only to recognise a £0.5 million provision for the ongoing monitoring costs over the next year and a contingent liability beyond that in these financial statements.  They also agreed to keep their assessment under review to take account of any changes in circumstances.(ii) and (iii)  These were properly decisions for the NIEA and Departmental Accounting Officers and not for ministers.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether she will place a copy of the notes that her officials took when meeting with Peoples Republic of China officials, on her latest visit, in the Assembly library.
answer_text: Written records of meetings are retained in accordance with the Department’s policy on the retention and disposal of records as per the requirements of the Functioning of Government (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act (Northern Ireland) 2021.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any legal mechanism that allows the Strategic Investment Board (SIB) to make Financial Transactional Capital loans, when the Comptroller and Auditor General’s Report on Financial Audit Findings 2024 – Central Government states that the SIB does not have vires to do so.
answer_text: The Strategic Investment Board (SIB) is a limited company established under powers contained in the Strategic Investment and Regeneration of Sites (NI) Order 2003.  The ability to issue loans falls within SIB’s general powers under its Articles and Memorandum of Association.  The Comptroller and Auditor General’s report recognises that SIB has this generic capability to issue loans.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) any direction she has given to Invest NI on investment decisions on the Peoples Republic of China, in light of the United Nations Human Rights 2022 report on its assessment of human rights concerns in the   Xinjiang Uyghur Autonomous Region, and also by the US Department of State in its China 2024 Human Rights Report, which reported genocide and crimes against humanity occurred during the year in China against predominantly Muslim Uyghurs and members of other ethnic and religious minority groups in Xinjiang, both of which have received internat
answer_text: My Written Ministerial Statement of 16 October made clear that companies seeking grant support will be expected to confirm that they are not manufacturing arms or components for any country that is found to be committing genocide. I also confirmed that my Department is developing an Ethical Investment Framework that will apply consistently across all countries and inform future investment decisions.The measures under development will be countryneutral, principlesbased, and apply equally to all jurisdictions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) any actions she is taking to ensure that there is no repeat of Northern Ireland Tourist Board irregular expenditure; and (ii) who is being held accountable for the irregular audit circumstances, given her Department's and its arm's-length bodies' accounts being disclaimed.
answer_text: Irregular expenditure in Tourism NI’s 2023-24 accounts related to the Statutory Certification Scheme not being funded at full cost by the industry. Tourism NI, in partnership with the Department, has commissioned a review of the cost and fees for the Statutory Certification Scheme. The Department continues to engage with Tourism NI on a regular basis to ensure all expenditure is regular and compliant.The 2023-24 Tourism NI accounts were not disclaimed and received an unqualified audit opinion.  This issue had no bearing on the Department for the Economy Group Accounts being disclaimed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail, in light of the Comptroller and Auditor General's findings (i) whether a review of the accounting and allocation of Financial Transactional Capital (FTC) will be undertaken; and (ii) the reason FTC assets and liabilities are reported on within the Executive group accounts.
answer_text: The Strategic Investment Board (SIB) has a legal capability to make loans or equity investments. As the Department of Finance (DoF) and Department for the Economy (DfE) do not have this power, in order to facilitate the use of the available FTC funding, this funding is currently being channelled through SIB, via The Executive Office (TEO).  My Department is seeking new legal powers for both DoF and DfE, to ensure both Departments have the required legal vires to issue and account for FTC funding. These legislative clauses have been included in the Administrative and Financial Provisions Bill, a Bill which was introduced to the Assembly on 23 June 2025 and is currently being considered by the Finance Committee. Should the Bill become enacted by the Assembly, the existing FTC loans being facilitated by TEO, will move to their parent Departments, thereby removing the technical qualification from the TEO accounts.  FTC funding can only be used to provide a loan to a private sector entity or take an equity share in a private entity. As indicated above, both DoF and DfE do not currently have the power to issue loans and, in order to facilitate the use of FTC funding by these departments, this is channelled by SIB, via TEO.  In relation to the accounting treatment, to ensure that these loans are reflected in public sector accounts, DoF directed SIB to recognise financial assets for these FTC loans which are now consolidated in TEO’s Statement of Financial Position. However, the assets and loans do not meet the government reporting manual (FReM) recognition criteria. This has resulted in technical qualification of the audit opinion. The technical qualification of TEO consolidated accounts results from the lead Departments (namely DoF and DfE) not having the legal capability to make loans or equity instruments. As highlighted, this will be addressed through the Administrative and Financial Provisions Bill.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) any (a) disciplinary; and (b) restorative, action her Department is taking, following her Department's accounts being disclaimed by the Comptroller and Auditor General for a second time.
answer_text: Both the Department and the Northern Ireland Audit Office (NIAO) have already confirmed that removing a disclaimer of opinion on a set of accounts is a multi-year process.  The issues leading to the disclaimer in the 2024/25 group accounts arise primarily from the 2023/24 balances which were disclaimed,  issues in in respect of the Northern Regional College (NRC) in relation to failure to provide adequate supporting documentation or appropriate audit evidence to support the figures provided to the department, and the valuation of the future repayments of student loans.  They do not call into question value for money, propriety or how the Department managed its budget.  The 2024/25 accounts were certified and laid within statutory deadlines, reflecting progress, and further improvement work continues. With the exception of student loans and NRC’s figures, the core Department and its 20 arm’s-length bodies (components) reported accurate figures, marking a significant improvement on the previous year. (i)(a) Disciplinary action:No disciplinary action has been initiated against departmental staff in connection with the audit disclaimers.  Neither the Northern Ireland Audit Office nor the Department of Finance has raised concerns about staff competence.  The challenge relates to the scale and complexity of consolidating a large and diverse group, which is believed to be one of the most complex group consolidations in Britain and the North of Ireland due to differing accounting frameworks, systems, reporting requirements, and different year ends.  The group’s diverse components include Further Education colleges and entities with cross-border operations, some reporting in euro, alongside technical areas such as student loan valuations, investment properties, and derivative instruments.  This diversity, combined with the need for inter-group eliminations and compliance with government standards, creates significant challenges for the consolidation of all these accounts.  As each component operates its own accounting system the group consolidation has to be done offline which, in itself, adds a further challenge.(i)(b) Restorative (remedial) action:A comprehensive programme of improvement has been in place from the 2022/23 financial year delivering improvements including: strengthened financial reporting capacity and quality assurance; earlier and clearer engagement with the 20  components within the  group accounting boundary; updated group reporting instructions and audit pack checklists; refinements to the consolidation process and timetable, including interim accounts to identify issues earlier and targeted support and oversight for higher risk components.  The improvement projects for the current year include work to update student loan modelling, further automating the Statement of Outturn against Assembly Supply, agreement of intercompany balances across components at interim stage, review of all accrual balances to ensure sufficient audit evidenc

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline the number of cases of non-fatal strangulation that have reached the Crown Court compared to a Magistrates' Court in 2025.
answer_text: ANSWER – AQO 2792/22-27Information from Departmental datasets and from NICTS management information systems indicate that, from January 2025 up to the end of October 2025, there had been 319 cases, excluding appeals, involving a non-fatal strangulation or asphyxiation offence, prosecuted under the Justice (Sexual Offences and Trafficking Victims) Act (NI) 2022, dealt with at court.  Of these, 51 were dealt with in Crown Court and 268 were dealt with in magistrates’ courts.In addition to these cases, at the end of October 2025, there were 97 of ongoing non-fatal strangulation cases still live in Crown Court and 263 in adult and youth magistrates’ courts.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) the devolved competencies of the NI Executive; and (ii) under what vires the Minister for the Economy can claim, contrary to the Northern Ireland Act 1998, non-transferred powers and competency in reserved matters, namely matters of defence, international relations and international trade.

To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any discussions she has had with trade unions, including Unite the Union, in relation to Northern Ireland’s defence industry, in light of the union's statement regarding the defence review in June 2025, on the need to translate defence spending into British growth, British Jobs, British Skills, British Innovation.
answer_text: I have not discussed the Strategic Defence Review, which is the responsibility of the British Government, with trade unions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, in relation to her comments in the Assembly on 17 November 2025 in respect of the defence industry, whether her Department has carried out any assessment on the potential impact  of any objections and sanctions from the United States of America against her Department, as to that nation being classified as a potential supporter of her definition of genocide.
answer_text: We enjoy a strong and positive relationship with the United States, both in terms of economic ties and broader cooperation in areas such as education and research. I am committed to maintaining and strengthening these important links while upholding the highest standards of ethical investment and human rights.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, in relation to the North South Interconnector, to detail (i) whether extra land will be vested to ensure space is made available for the extra 12 cables and trenches required, against overhead installation; (ii) the subsidy mechanism she is considering, given undergrounding costs are in the order of ten times more expensive; and (iii) the environmental impact of (a) additional cabling trenches; (b) disturbances to areas of outstanding natural beauty and wildlife; and (c) the construction of step up and step down transformers at either end to the Northern Ir
