answer_text: At present, SONI and NIE Networks do not plan to vest or acquire additional land for extra cable works. As a result, no vesting applications have been submitted. Since no extra cable works are planned, there will be no further environmental impacts. There are currently no subsidies in place for underground cables.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when they will answer AQW 32266/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 32266/22-27 was answered on 19 December 2025.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister how they propose to implement the recommendations outlined in the Independent Review of the Windsor Framework by the Rt Hon Lord Murphy.

To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 33821/22-27, to detail (i) the rationale for her direction to Invest NI when, our nation, which retains competency in accordance with the Northern Ireland Act 1998 for foreign policy, defence and international trade, has concluded that Israel is not committing genocide in Gaza, according to the letter from the Foreign Secretary to the Chair of the UK Parliament’s International Development Committee on 1 September 2025, and thereby giving de jure recognition of UK law and policy; (ii) the legal grounds she has for not complying with the UK Go
answer_text: My decision is firmly grounded in international and domestic law. In its report published on 16 September, the UN Independent Commission of Inquiry on the Occupied Palestinian Territory, including East Jerusalem, and Israel found that Israel has committed genocide against Palestinians in Gaza. Genocide is abhorrent and illegal under international law. 	This reinforces the International Court of Justice advisory opinion of July 2024, which concluded that Israel’s continued occupation of Palestinian territory is unlawful and that states have an obligation not to recognise or assist in maintaining that situation, and the UN General Assembly resolution of 18 September 2024 which followed from this.		This resolution reflects a broad international consensus that states should not facilitate violations of international law. My written statement of 16 October addresses matters within my legislative competence, specifically trade facilitation and economic development under the current framework and the transferred responsibility for the furtherance of trade under the NI Act 1998. While foreign policy and defence remain reserved matters, Schedule 3 of the Act provides powers for advancing trade and economic development within devolved competence. My actions are consistent with these powers and do not contravene British law. Rather, they reflect obligations under international law that have been incorporated into British legislation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) whether she will engage with Translink Senior Management regarding the consultations Translink had with Ballyclare High School leadership and the PSNI Road Policing Unit, in respect of its new bus arrangements, that have led to 300 pupils, having to walk along narrow pavements and poorly lit roads; (ii) for her assessment of Translink's risk register on these new arrangements, with its enhanced challenges to pupils, especially in light of the forthcoming winter conditions, including non-gritted pavements; and (iii) to detail who is responsible within
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:Since this question was raised a multiagency meeting took place on the 21st November 2025. A further option of the use of Foundry Lane to access the Doagh Road, adjacent to the school grounds, was tabled for consideration. This option was collectively explored 8 years ago, however an issue with land ownership at that time prevented progressing it’s use. It is believed this is no longer an issue. Therefore, Translink is committed to working collaboratively with Ballyclare High School, the EA, DfI Roads and Policing to re-assess the suitability of this option for use. This assessment will be conducted in the coming weeks. In the interim period, the arrangements in place at Readers Park will remain.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, following her Written Ministerial Statement on the Invest NI Review of Investments on 16 October 2025, (i) to detail the rationale for instructing her Department to break (a) Annex 2 of the Northern Ireland Protocol, namely Regulation (EU) 2015/479; Regulation (EU) 2015/936; Obligations stemming from the international agreements concluded by the Union; and (b) the Euro-Mediterranean Agreement, establishing an association between the European Communities and their Member States, of the one part, and the State of Israel; and (ii) to detail the rational for no
answer_text: Article 5 and Annex 2 of the Windsor Framework preserves a cohort of EU laws which continue to have application in the North. None of the EU laws specified in Annex 2 require the provision of public funding for the support of manufacturing of arms or components to be used in genocidal actions. There has been no breach of Annex 2.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any notification they have received from the Office of the Northern Ireland Executive in Brussels with respect to the Windsor Framework, regarding (i) current dental amalgam supply issues; and (ii) the potential impact of the forthcoming Conference of the Parties in Brazil, on the supply of dental amalgam.
answer_text: We have not received any notification from the Office of the Northern Ireland Executive in Brussels regarding current dental amalgam supply issues.  We have asked officials to investigate this issue.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any actions they are taking to resolve issues surrounding the Windsor Framework, in light of Lord Murphy's recent report on the Windsor Framework, and the House of Lords report on the Windsor Framework and the Northern Ireland Protocol.
answer_text: We are reviewing the recommendations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether an assessment has been made on the effects to the agriculture sector in the event of no agreement on the provisions of veterinary medicines between the EU and UK.
answer_text: In line with the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, the supply of veterinary medicines to Northern Ireland, including any assessment of the potential effects of a ‘no agreement’ scenario between the EU/UK on veterinary medicines provision here, falls under the Direction and Control of the Secretary of State for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs (Defra).I will continue to engage with the UK Government on the need to ensure the continued supply of veterinary medicines to Northern Ireland beyond the end of the grace period on 31 December 2025.I welcome the UK Government’s publication of the Protecting Animal Health policy in June 2025, which offers practical solutions to safeguard veterinary medicine supplies into Northern Ireland.  This policy underpins the introduction of two new support schemes, designed to be responsive, adaptable, and flexible in addressing both anticipated and unforeseen supply challenges.I believe these schemes represent a sustainable and forward-looking approach, marking a significant milestone in securing the future of our local veterinary medicines supply.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any steps he is taking to ensure that any additional Barnett consequential funding from the forthcoming budget is allocated to the pay award for health and social care staff.
answer_text: Any Barnett consequentials arising from the Autumn Budget will be allocated by the Executive in line with agreed priorities. The Executive has agreed that, after honouring the £7 million commitment to the PSNI, the Departments of Health and Education will have first call on up to £100 million and £37 million respectively of funding available for health service and teachers’ pay costs in the December Monitoring round. The Departments for Infrastructure and Justice will also have first call on up to £6.7 million each for pay pressures.  This demonstrates the Executive’s clear priority to support health and other public sector workers, and I will continue to work closely with the Health Minister and Executive colleagues to secure the necessary funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any engagement between her Department or Invest NI and UK or US Officials regarding (i) foreign; (ii) defence; or (iii) international trade, in respect of (a) aerospace; and (b) defence, manufacturing, since her Written Ministerial Statement and direction on the Invest NI Review of Investments on 16 October 2025.
answer_text: My officials maintain regular engagement with their counterparts in the Department for Business and Trade on a range of international trade matters, on a cross-sectoral basis.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, further to her Written Ministerial Statement on 16 October 2025 on Invest NI Review of Investments, (i) whether any assessment has been made of the economic impact if the defence and aerospace businesses where to relocate from Northern Ireland; and (ii) to detail the plans she has to replace the (a) current £2 billion in GDP; and (b) several thousand jobs.
answer_text: There is no evidence that businesses will relocate as a result of the measures I have announced.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the reasons (i) their office has not provided approval for the Office of the Northern Ireland Executive in Brussels to brief the Windsor Framework Democratic Scrutiny Committee (DSC) on the forthcoming changes to the Union Customs Code; and (ii) they have not taken the recommendations from Lord Murphy's recent report on the Windsor Framework, or from the House of Lords report on the Windsor Framework, both of which noted the paucity of information being provided to MLAs through the DSC.
answer_text: Approval has been provided for the Office of the Northern Ireland Executive in Brussels to brief the Windsor Framework Democratic Scrutiny Committee (DSC).

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any actions they are taking to understand the application of the forthcoming revised European Union Union Customs Code to businesses and to inform them of any potential impact.
answer_text: The Department for the Economy (DfE) is taking the lead on this matter and has provided the Windsor Framework Democratic Scrutiny Committee with its current analysis of the impact of the revised EU Union Customs Code. The Office of the Northern Ireland Executive in Brussels (ONIEB) has been monitoring progress of the proposal to revise the Code through the EU legislative system.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, further to her Written Ministerial Statement on 16 October 2025 on Invest NI Review of Investments, whether she gave a Ministerial Direction to the (i) Permanent Secretary; and (ii) Chief Executive Officer of Invest NI.
answer_text: Section 4 of the Departments Order 1999 states that ‘the functions of a department shall at all times be exercised subject to the direction and control of the Minister’, which establishes the normal position. Separately, Managing Public Money NI sets out the process whereby an Accounting Officer may seek a formal written direction in relation to a policy, project or programme. No such direction was sought or issued in relation to my Written Ministerial Statement on 16 October 2025.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, in relation to the comments by the First Minister in the Assembly Chamber on 20 October, that defence, foreign policy, and international trade are reserved matters, to detail whether they will consider referring the Minister for the Economy's direction to Invest NI to the full Executive, in relation to her Written Ministerial Statement on 16 October.
answer_text: There is currently no joint Executive Office position on referring this issue to the Executive for consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, further to her Written Ministerial Statement on 16 October 2025 on Invest NI Review of Investments, to detail (i) the definition of genocide that she has instructed her Department to use; and (ii) whether this is an agreed definition with His Majesty's Government, who has responsibility for foreign, defence and trade affairs.
answer_text: The definition of genocide is laid out in the United Nations 1948 Convention on the Prevention and Punishment of the Crime of Genocide (the Genocide Convention). The British Government incorporated the Convention into legislation by virtue of the International Criminal Court Act 2001, which extends to England, Wales and the North.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, further to her Written Ministerial Statement on 16 October 2025 on Invest NI Review of Investments, to detail any discussions her Department has had with the (i) Foreign, Commonwealth and Development Office; and (ii) Department for Business and Trade, given that her statement involves issues of foreign policy and international trade.
answer_text: My written statement of the 16 October concerns matters over which I have legislative competence. This is in accordance with the powers of trade facilitation and economic development under the current legislative framework, and the transferred responsibility for the furtherance of trade under the Northern Ireland Act 1998.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, further to her Written Ministerial Statement on 16 October 2025 on Invest NI Review of Investments, to detail the legal grounds on which she can instruct a government body, in particular Invest NI, not to engage in UK overseas trade negotiations.
answer_text: Trade negotiations are led by the Department for Business and Trade (DBT) – my Department, not Invest NI engages directly with DBT. Section 4 of the Departments Order 1999 states that ‘the functions of a department shall at all times be exercised subject to the direction and control of the Minister.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, further to her Written Ministerial Statement on 16 October 2025 on Invest NI Review of Investments, to detail the legal authority she has to instruct Invest NI on foreign, defence or trade policy, which are not devolved matters.
answer_text: My Written Statement relates to functions within my control. Section 4 of the Departments Order 1999 states that ‘the functions of a department shall at all times be exercised subject to the direction and control of the Minister’. The Industrial Development Act (NI) 2002 provides the legal basis for my Department to ensure Invest NI operates in accordance with its priorities.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any assessment that has been made of the Minister for the Economy's Written Ministerial Statement on 16 October 2025 on Invest NI Review of Investments on (i) conducting foreign, defence and trade policy that are not devolved matters; and (ii) the impact on USA/NI relations by referencing defence manufactured goods being used by the USA in its military operations in the Middle East.
answer_text: We have not undertaken any joint assessment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) the amount spent, to date, on the NOVA project; (ii) whether there are any penalties to the Northern Ireland Civil Service for ending the NOVA contract; and (iii) the reporting made by his Department on the NOVA project, including to whom and when.
answer_text: ANSWERThe NOVA Programme was established in 2019 to initiate and co-ordinate multiple projects to replace a number of legacy ICT systems. The key deliverables from NOVA have now been delivered and the programme has closed. Total spend on the NOVA Programme is £10.05M. A review of the transformation agenda in Land & Property Services (LPS) has been undertaken and a new agile approach developed which will support and enable the change required to deliver an integrated modern LPS focused on customer’s needs. The pace and scale of change will be dependent upon amongst other factors the funding available.No penalties have been incurred in relation to the NOVA Programme.  In April 2025, governance arrangements were reviewed to replace the NOVA Programme Board with a Strategic Change Board, chaired by the LPS Deputy Secretary to oversee the delivery of LPS transformation.  This reports quarterly through the LPS Portfolio, Programme and Project Office (P3O) to the Department of Finance Departmental Board, as is standard for all major programmes.  Where appropriate, ad hoc reporting is provided to other government bodies on request, e.g. NIAO.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether the NOVA project had been cancelled.
answer_text: ANSWERI refer the member to AQW 33714/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in light of House of Lords Northern Ireland Scrutiny Committee's October 2025 report Northern Ireland after Brexit: Strengthening Northern Ireland’s voice in the context of the Windsor Framework, whether he has (i) reviewed his assessment that faithful implementation best suits local agribusiness; (ii) given consideration to the implications on a delay of a SPS agreement until 2027 at earliest to local agribusiness; and (iii) reviewed his Department's approach to enforcement of EU rules, given the report's observations on the d
answer_text: Under the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, some elements of the Windsor Framework, including the movement of agri-food goods and associated official controls, fall under the direction and control of the Secretary of State for the Environment, Food and Rural Affairs. Nevertheless, I want to see a SPS agreement reached with the EU as quickly as possible with the maximum possible level of dynamic alignment with EU legislation in order to reduce trade friction.  Until a SPS Agreement is reached, current Windsor Framework requirements continue to apply and for the areas under my direction and control I remain committed to meeting our legal obligations through full, faithful and timely implementation of the Windsor Framework.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any engagement her Department has had with aerospace industry bodies on creating a bespoke aerospace defence apprenticeship training scheme in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The ApprenticeshipsNI and Higher-Level Apprenticeships programmes support talent development across a wide range of occupational areas, from Level 2 and Level 3 apprenticeships through to higher level qualifications. These include apprenticeships in areas such as engineering, mechatronics, logistics, ICT and procurement and supply.The Advanced Manufacturing and Engineering Sectoral Partnership works with the aerospace industry to review current apprenticeship frameworks, identify and introduce any new frameworks and source relevant qualifications to meet the needs of the sector in line with demand for skills.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on actions being taken to address clinical variation across Northern Ireland.

To ask the Minister for the Economy for a breakdown of (i) the minimum and maximum costs of her proposed closure of the Non-Domestic Renewable Heat Incentive Scheme to the budget; (ii) the minimum and maximum costs of her proposed closure of the Scheme in Annually Managed Expenditure; and (iii) the costs for these amounts, for each year, over the lifespan of the Scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline any engagement his Department is having with Science, Technology, Engineering and Mathematics champions in light of proposals around AS-levels.
answer_text: My Department’s consultation on the future of CCEA GCSEs, AS levels and A levels is an opportunity for students, parents, educators, employers and wider stakeholders to contribute by sharing their views on how we can improve the structure, content and assessment of these qualifications. The consultation is also an opportunity for stakeholders, such as those from the STEM community, to share their views on my proposals to ensure that CCEA’s qualifications remain relevant and fit for purpose.  The proposal to remove AS levels will not have a detrimental impact upon the range of A level STEM subjects on offer to students in Northern Ireland. The removal of AS levels will reduce the pressure on students and teachers and free up time for teaching and deeper learning. My proposals reflect a clear ambition to ensure that CCEA GCSEs, AS levels and A levels remain relevant both nationally and internationally. Following the consultation, I will give consideration to the responses and all other relevant factors and empirical evidence. After this, as part of the revision process for new CCEA GCSEs and GCEs, CCEA will engage with subject experts.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the exact (a) PSNI personnel numbers; and (b) retirements, on a six monthly basis, from 2010 to the present day; and (ii) when, during this period, her Department produced PSNI recovery plans, that are reportedly operational matters under the remit of the Chief Constable of Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department neither collates nor holds this information.  The Member may wish to visit PSNI’s website (Strength of Police Service Statistics | PSNI) which provides a breakdown of the number of officers currently in post or contact the PSNI directly. While my Department is not responsible for producing PSNI recovery plans, it is important to note that during the previous mandate when I was in office, police officer numbers were increased to 7,100.  It is deeply regrettable that the subsequent suspension of the political institutions contributed to a significant decline in those numbers.  My department also worked to support the Chief Constable in producing the latest PSNI Workforce Recovery business case which I approved on value for money grounds and I remain firmly committed to supporting its implementation, subject to Executive agreeing the funds.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 31983/22-7 and AQW 31979/22-7, for a further updated assessment on the security and continuity of electricity supply, in light of the urgent NI Executive meeting and the briefing to the Committee for the Economy by the Department for the Economy's Permanent Secretary, on the amber alert.
answer_text: I refer to my statement of 1 October 2025, providing an update on the electricity supply for the North.  There are no further updates.Department for the Economy - UPDATE ON ELECTRICITY SUPPLY

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline her Department's assessment for implications to the manufacturing sector, from EU steel quotas.
answer_text: I am aware that the EU has presented a proposal which it intends to replace its current steel safeguard measures from July 2026. I welcome the Commission’s commitment to continue to apply the same regime agreed with the British Government as far as trade flows from Britain into the North are concerned, in full respect of the Windsor Framework. As it begins the process of setting the specific volumes associated with the proposed Regulation, I will take every opportunity to encourage the EU to engage with the British Government to ensure the position here in the north is not undermined. I have already raised the issue directly with Ambassador Susanne Baumann, for example, and will raise in upcoming engagements with British Government Ministers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any action his Department is taking to prevent the repeated fish kills in the Six Mile River, South Antrim.
answer_text: Preventing fish kills is a shared responsibility across all sectors that impact water quality. My department through the Lough Neagh Action Plan, has been able to increase inspection staffing numbers to carry out 190 science-led investigations in the Six Mile Water catchment to date. Referrals are made to the appropriate teams for follow-up actions, where issues are found. My department operates a 24/7 water pollution hotline for the public to report suspected water pollution for further investigation.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline (i) when he expects the border inspection centres in (a) Larne; (b) Belfast; (c) Foyle; and (d) Warrenpoint, to be fully operational; (ii) what costs have been incurred by his Department to facilitate this; (iii) what draw down in resources his Department has made from the UK Government; and (iv) whether this is accounted for as DEL or AME expenditure.
answer_text: As a result of the Official Controls (Northern Ireland) Regulations 2023, the construction, associated costs and funding of Northern Ireland Sanitary and Phytosanitary (SPS) Inspection Facilities is a function of the Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs (Defra).  These Facilities have now been handed over to DAERA, though it should be noted that under the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, activities at these Facilities fall under the direction and control of the Defra Secretary of State.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline the qualifications his Department has to process applications for marine licensing.
answer_text: The Department has statutory authority under Part 4 of the Marine and Coastal Access Act 2009 to process marine licence applications within Northern Ireland’s inshore waters. This function is carried out by the Marine and Fisheries Division.		The Division is comprised of staff recruited through the Northern Ireland Civil Service (NICS), based on relevant qualifications and experience in marine / environmental science, environmental regulation, and licensing procedures.		Staff typically hold degrees or equivalent qualifications in Environmental Science, Marine Biology, Geography, or related disciplines, and are appointed through structured, competitive recruitment processes that assess technical knowledge, analytical skills, and regulatory experience. 		Marine licensing officers apply the Marine Licensing Operating Principles and assess and determine applications in line with relevant environmental legislation.  		The Department also ensures staff are trained on licensing and enforcement procedures to support robust and transparent decision-making.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail whether he plans to (i) explain; and (ii) publish, a full set of rules and regulations for the use of Financial transactions capital, so that other organisations can avail of the revised understanding of loan and equity accounting, as explained by the Minister, in relation to Casement Park.
answer_text: The rules governing Financial Transactions Capital (FTC) are outlined in both the Treasury’s Consolidated Budgeting Guidance and Statement of Funding Policy and has not changed from the position set out in these documents.However, the Treasury has revised the method by which FTC is allocated to the Executive. Following the recent Spending Review, it was confirmed that FTC is now provided solely on a net basis. This means the allocation already accounts for loan repayments, and as such, no repayments to the Treasury are required. This allows the Executive to recycle FTC receipts indefinitely.My officials are currently reviewing the Department of Finance’s FTC guidance for departments and will consider updates if necessary.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline whether (i) historic flood risk mapping has been conducted; and (ii) drain and watercourse dredging in risk areas, has been prioritised.
answer_text: My Department’s Rivers Directorate have produced historic flood maps that illustrate areas that are known to have flooded in the past and they are available to the public on the Flood Maps NI web viewer: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/topics/flood-maps-ni. My Department also undertakes a rolling programme of inspections on designated watercourses to identify maintenance requirements to help alleviate the impacts of flooding. Where inspections identify issues that significantly interfere with the efficiency or flow of a watercourse, and subject to the availability of resources, these works are added to the Rivers Operations works programme.Maintenance works may include vegetation management, channel clearance, and the removal of materials or obstructions that could significantly impede the free flow of water.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any forward planning, or horizon scanning, that has been conducted on the forthcoming revised European Union Union Customs Code.
answer_text: Customs is not a devolved matter, but there are potential implications here. The Department for the Economy (DfE) is taking the lead on this matter and have provided the Windsor Framework Democratic Scrutiny Committee with its current analysis of the impact of the revised Code. The Office of the Northern Ireland Executive in Brussels (ONIEB) has been monitoring progress of the proposal to revise the EU’s Union Customs Code through the EU legislative system.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any surveillance his Department has introduced to ensure that the UK Internal Market is protected from sub standard or harmful agriculture goods and products entering NI from the EU via the Irish Republic.
answer_text: In line with the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, the movement of agricultural goods and products to and from Northern Ireland falls under the direction and control of the Secretary of State for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs how his Department will ensure travellers, hauliers and businesses are aware of the rules on meat imports, including the risk of disease introduction and associated penalties.
answer_text: Under the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, the import of meat into Northern Ireland, including how the rules are publicised, is a matter under the direction and control of the Secretary of State for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, pursuant to AQW 28004/22-27, to detail (i) who is the line manager of the Head of Civil Service; (a) in which documentation the awarding of her performance related pay bonuses is laid out; and (b) whether they will lay same documentation in the NI Assembly Library; (ii) the criteria used by the line manager of the Head of Civil Service to establish that a performance related pay award was warranted; and (iii) the timescales involved in relation to the comment any given point in time.
answer_text: As this is a policy issue upon which the Department of Finance (DoF) has responsibility DoF have provided the following information.DoF has confirmed that the position of HoCS has no line manager structure or reporting officer or countersigning officer to undertake the normal appraisal process as provided for under the NICS Performance Management policy. This matter is under review.The HOCS role is not subject to performance related bonuses; non-consolidated performance related bonuses were removed for senior civil servants here, including the HOCS role in 2009, with new pay scales introduced as part of the Senior Civil Service pay strategy in 2012. The HOCS role is subject to the pay award applied to all other Civil Service posts, ie:  HOCS pay increase reflects the agreed 20-month Civil Service 2024-25 pay award applied to all civil servants comprising progression plus 3% revalorisation of pay scales from 1 August 2024 and progression plus 6% revalorisation from 1 August 2025.  Civil Service pay awards have been applied to the role of HOCS in this way since the introduction of the Senior Civil Service pay strategy in 2012.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of savings being made in agency and locum costs.
answer_text: A Regional Group has been established to co-ordinate the improvement of staff utilisation, with the aim of reducing unsustainable agency and locum spend. Significant progress has already been made towards the cessation of ‘Off-Framework’ agency use with expenditure falling from £134m in 22/23 to £14m in 24/25 across Nursing & Midwifery and Social Workers. For these two staff groups, the overall reduction in agency expenditure in 2024/25 compared to the base year (2022/23), was £25m.In addressing the Medical & Dental locum costs work is already underway including implementation of a new Medical & Dental locum framework by the end of 2025; improved Medical & Dental workforce planning; development of regional guidance for the utilisation and monitoring of locums; and a revision of the rates payable under the current HSC e-locum service.Stabilisation and growth of our workforce is also central to our ambitions to reduce agency use and the significant progress achieved in this area in recent years must be acknowledged with over 10,000 more whole time equivalent staff directly employed by the HSC now compared to in 2018.  I very much welcome the ongoing progress that has been made across HSC in relation to reducing agency usage especially at a time when the health service is facing a period of unprecedented budgetary pressures. HSC staff have been clear that excessive agency use is unfair and impacts on morale and more importantly, having consistent clinical teams also enhances patient safety.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of the reliability of generating capacity in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Security of supply is a key priority for my Department, and we work closely with the System Operator (SONI), the Utility Regulator (UR) and the wider energy industry to review and monitor security of supply and ensure the resilience of the electricity system.Our three large fossil fuel generating stations, renewable generation, and interconnectors each play a part in delivering secure electricity supplies.Current operating standards require large fossil fuel generation in at least two separate locations at all times. This allows the system to operate securely during unplanned incidents or generator outages.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Departmental risk register identifies EU emissions regulations as a threat to the current security of energy supply.
answer_text: Responsibility for security of energy supply rests with the Department for the Economy (DfE). My Department’s Corporate Risk Register (CRR) is regularly reviewed and updated. It identifies and outlines strategic risks that could potentially impact the Department’s ability to operate effectively and achieve its core objectives. As of the latest version in October 2025, the CRR does not include EU emissions regulations as a current threat to the security of energy supply among its identified strategic risks.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the impacts EU emission regulations have had on (i) short-term; (ii) medium-term; and (iii) long-term energy planning.
answer_text: I refer to my statement of 1 October 2025, providing an update on the electricity supply for the North.  The mitigations were due to the exceptional outages that impacted Ballylumford and Coolkerragh power stations in 2025. Department for the Economy - UPDATE ON ELECTRICITY SUPPLY . Security of supply is a key priority for my Department, and we work closely with the System Operator (SONI), the Utility Regulator (UR) and the wider energy industry to review and monitor security of supply and ensure the resilience of the electricity system.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, in relation to the Holding the Irish Government to Account on Legacy motion in the Assembly on 23 September 2025, and her subsequent comments on the Nolan Show on 24 September 2025 regarding victims and the perpetrators of violence, (i) whether this is the position of the Department of Justice; (ii) for her assessment of how these comments comply with Article 2 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms; and (iii) whether she has had any discussions with the Attorney General on these points.
answer_text: I did not speak or vote in the debate on the Assembly motion. Nothing I said on the Nolan Show is in conflict with the position of the Department of Justice, or Article 2 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms. I have had no discussions with the Attorney General on these points.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of the security and continuity of electricity supplies in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I refer to my statement of 1 October 2025, providing an update on the electricity supply for the North.Department for the Economy - UPDATE ON ELECTRICITY SUPPLY

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail whether the deputy First Minister had the opportunity to promote immigration and customs preclearance for the United States of America at Belfast International Airport in talks with US Officials, prior to her recent meeting with the President of the United States.
answer_text: We are fully supportive of the reintroduction of a transatlantic route to Belfast and implementation of pre-clearance. A direct flight with pre-clearance between here and the USA would have transformative economic benefits for our economy, and we took the opportunity to raise that during our meeting with the US Ambassador to the UK in July.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether she has directed Invest NI to promote Northern Ireland, as the part of this Island, that has the advantage of UK and US trade tariff advantage over the Republic of Ireland.
answer_text: While I always promote this region's strengths and advantages, I would advise caution at this stage in relation to tariffs. All exporters to the US are in a worse position as a result of the tariffs. In addition, there is still important detail to be confirmed and clarified. The arrangements agreed by the US Administration with both the British Government and the EU are, at this stage, non-binding and subject to further negotiations. Furthermore, comparing headline average tariff rates overlooks complexities around rules of origin and ‘tariff stacking’.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment on the advantages the £31 billion Tech Prosperity Deal, will have for Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Invest NI will continue to pitch the north’s technological, research and industrial capabilities to the North American market and beyond.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the work her Department is undertaking to grow the defence sector in Northern Ireland, following the DESI UK 2025 Conference in London.
answer_text: My department’s sectoral action plans set out the key activities being taken forward by it and its delivery partners, to help grow the most innovative, internationally-orientated and productive parts of the economy in the North, including the advanced manufacturing sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline any action he can take to reduce pressures on teaching principals in the primary school sector.
answer_text: As part of the 2024/25 teachers’ pay settlement, I commissioned an independent panel to undertake a Review of Teacher Workload issues. The panel has been established and has been tasked with examining all aspects of teacher workload, as well as monitoring progress with the implementation of a series of workload commitments that were part of the pay settlement. The panel has been meeting with stakeholders, including teaching principals, to get an understanding of the workload issues and will be submitting a report to me in November with a number of recommendations.In addition to the Independent Review of Teacher Workload, in reaching agreement on pay in April 2025 the Northern Ireland Teaching Council (NITC) and Management Side of the Teachers’ Negotiating Committee (TNC) negotiated a Heads of Agreement which form the agreed basis on which teachers’ and school leaders’ workload issues will be addressed going forward. The focus of TNC is to address and implement the workload commitments detailed in the Heads of Agreement in the best interests of all teachers and school leaders including teaching principals in the primary school sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the representation she has made to her officials, to ensure that her Department provides appropriate and correct consultee information at (a) public inquiries; and (b) in the courts; and (ii) whether any disciplinary action was taken against her officials for their failure to provide the appropriate documentation to (a) the Dalradian Goldmine Public Local Inquiry; and (b) the courts, in respect of the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme.
answer_text: My officials always seek to provide all necessary and requested information to the Planning Appeals Commission (PAC) and to the Courts. If any shortcoming is identified, I support an approach of ensuring that lessons are learned to ensure that any similar issues are avoided. For the Dalradian public local inquiry, the inquiry process allows for the identification of issues to allow them to be rectified to better inform the process. In this case, a procedural matter regarding transboundary consultation was raised in discussions and my Department committed to reissue the transboundary consultation to formally notify and consult the Irish Government as the European Economic Area state in order to fully satisfy the Environmental Impact Regulations. I believe public participation, transparency, and procedural fairness are key considerations in the planning process. With regard to the A5 Western Transport Corridor, my Department worked extremely closely over recent years with very experienced Senior and Junior Counsel, and in particular during the extensive development of my Department’s detailed response to the PAC recommendations, which addressed each of the recommendations.  Their advice was followed diligently at all times by my officials. The barristers also worked hand in hand with my officials in preparing the voluminous and complex documentation which was laid in front of both my predecessor and the Executive for consideration in taking the Decision to proceed with the scheme in October 2024. My officials also worked with other departments, where relevant, to address the recommendations.Therefore, I am satisfied that the appropriate documentation has been provided by Officials and that has helped inform my recent decision to appeal the judgment.I have no reason to question the actions taken or diligence and professionalism applied by Officials working on the A5.While my immediate priority remains the preparation of a robust appeal, the ultimate aim is to ensure the delivery of this flagship project, and I am determined to do all that I can to ensure that happens so that no more lives are lost.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, in light of the Chancellor of the Exchequer's statement on the use of the Treasury Reserve, to outline what contingencies she has made to pay for the PSNI (i) data breach compensation; and (ii) holiday pay issues.
answer_text: My Department is currently facing exceptional pressures of £228 million arising from legal claims, including the PSNI data breach and holiday pay issues. These costs are best estimates at this stage as there will be a range of potential costs depending on the eventual settlement of these legal claims. The estimated cost of £228 million represents approximately 16% of my Department’s current non-ringfenced Resource DEL budget of £1.4 billion for 2025-26. In addition, my Department reported stabilisation pressures of £32 million for 2025-26 in the recent June Monitoring Round.Given the quantum involved, the costs of settling the legal claims are not affordable from within the Department of Justice (DoJ) budget under any circumstance without additional funding from the Executive or HM Treasury. I will continue therefore to engage with the Minister of Finance and Executive colleagues to secure this vital funding in order to resolve these matters.My officials also continue to engage with their Department of Finance (DoF) counterparts and I understand that DoF officials remain in discussions with HM Treasury officials.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, in light of comments by the Chancellor of the Exchequer in relation to the £9 billion Treasury Reserve, to detail any Executive Departments that have maximised their savings and may be able to access this funding.
answer_text: ANSWERThe Treasury Reserve is intended to provide funding for unforeseen, unaffordable and unavoidable pressures.  You will be aware that Departments must live within the budgets allocated to them, and I recognise that, given the wider constraint on public expenditure, departments are making efficiencies and savings in order to do so. However, maximising savings in itself would not be sufficient for the Treasury to agree access to the Reserve.Where a need arises, I will always make the strongest possible case for access to the Reserve on behalf of departments. As such I have requested access to the Reserve for the costs of the PSNI Data breach, which I believe meets the criteria. However, the 9 September announcement that the Reserve is set to be halved this year raises concerns that identified pressures, will no longer receive support.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, following the work undertaken by DfI Roads on the Doagh, Hillhead and Ballyrobert Road areas, Ballyclare, whether capital released from the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme can be used to repair the Ballycorr Road in South Antrim.
answer_text: I have been advised that Ballycorr Road is included within my Department’s resurfacing programme.  However, delivery of this scheme will be subject to the substantial completion of two housing developments adjacent to the Ballycorr Road.  This decision has been reached due to the prospect of damaged caused by construction traffic, utility works and the further potential for a right turn lane on the Ballycorr Road, which will be dependent on the final number of housing units.In the meantime, officials will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with my Department’s policy.In relation to the A5 Western Transport Corridor, funding for this project is ringfenced as it is an Executive Flagship project, and therefore any allocation or reallocation will be a decision for the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, in light of the Chancellors announcement on the £9 billion Treasury Reserve, to detail what additional pressures this places on Departments seeking budget relief through the reserve.
answer_text: ANSWERThe Treasury Reserve is intended to provide funding for unforeseen, unaffordable and unavoidable pressures.  The Chancellor’s announcement advised that the Reserve, which was £9 billion last year, is set to be halved in 2025. It is concerning that the Treasury appears to have reduced the Reserve to such an extent that it may no longer be sufficient to cover such unavoidable costs.This will affect devolved governments as well as Whitehall departments.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, following the recent announcement of a merger between the universities of Greenwich and Kent, to detail (i) any consideration her Department has made towards savings in the Higher Education Sector; and (ii) what indicative savings could be made by merging the senior management roles of both Queen's University Belfast and Ulster University, passing those savings on for research and postgraduate student support.
answer_text: I am aware of the financial challenges in the higher education sector and am committed to ensuring our institutions are financially secure and sustainable both now and in the longer term. The first stage of the HE Funding Review, a Financial Needs Assessment of our Higher Education Institutions, is underway and due to be completed during the autumn.Queen’s University Belfast and Ulster University are separate autonomous institutions and are responsible for their own staffing structures. The Department has no legislative vires in this area and has given no consideration to indicative savings that could be made by merging the senior management roles of both institutions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline how returning vested land along the route of the A5 Western Transport Corridor is conducive to the continuation of the project.
answer_text: The A5WTC High Court judgment was delivered on Monday 23 June 2025. The Judge ruled that the Orders including the Vesting Order (VO) would be quashed. The Court Order was filed on 27 June 2025, and ownership of vested lands by law reverted to the original landowners with effect from that date. My Department had no discretion on this matter.This position remains unchanged despite the appeal and the land continues to be held by the original owners. The loss of access to the vested lands presents challenges in relation to retaining the advance works that had been carried out and the completion of environmental surveys My Department has written to landowners identifying the preferred approach which is to enter into a licence agreement with the landowners. This would allow my Department to retain the benefit of advance works and undertake necessary surveys, with appropriate compensation provided to the landowners. Whilst this is the preferred option, my Department will continue to work closely with landowners and their agents to seek to agree a way forward that reflects the current circumstances and legal context.My priority remains the safety and wellbeing of all road users, and I remain committed to delivering the A5WTC.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance when they will publish details of all performance-related pay for senior grade members of the Northern Ireland Civil Service, including (i) Grades 3-5; (ii) Permanent Secretaries; and (iii) Head of Civil Service.
answer_text: Pay scales for Senior Civil Service grades including Grades 3-5, Permanent Secretaries and the Head of Civil Service and Frequently Asked Questions relating to eligibility criteria are published here: 2024/25 Pay Award | Department of Finance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether they will publish criteria for performance pay for senior grade members of the Northern Ireland Civil Service, including (i) Grades 3-5; (ii) Permanent Secretaries; and (iii) Head of Civil Service.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the response for AQW 30118/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on latest expected in-service date for the North South Interconnector.
answer_text: The current delivery timeline for the North South Interconnector indicates an in-service date of quarter 4 2031.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the east to west LirIC Interconnector Project.
answer_text: In December 2024 the Utility Regulator granted a transmission license to Transmission Investment (TI) LIRIC Limited in respect of the proposed LirIC interconnector. This is the first stage of a two-step process, and the Utility Regulator will now move to assess financial provisions in respect of the licence. Officials in my Department continue to work with the Utility Regulator in respect of the proposed interconnector.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether she has engaged with the Ministry of Defence to promote Northern Ireland's shipbuilding and defence as sub-contractors for the recent £10 billion Type 26 frigates order from Norway.
answer_text: I have not had any engagement with the Ministry of Defence.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether the Chancellor of the Exchequer has indicated if further revenue raising powers will be made available to the Executive.
To ask the Minister of Education for an update on any progress on School Enhancement Programmes that have been put on hold in South Antrim.
answer_text: As a result of the focus on the development and delivery of the ten SEP projects at special schools, the Education Authority (EA) was experiencing some resourcing pressures in relation to the preparation of business cases for some mainstream SEP projects.  Unfortunately, this resulted in delays to a number of projects.  However, the EA has now identified additional resources and is making good progress in the development of SEP business cases.There are nine School Enhancement Programme projects advancing in design in South Antrim as follows:Antrim Primary School and Fairview Primary School are at RIBA Stage 4 – Technical Design.  This is the final design stage before procurement of a works contractor.The project at Ballyclare Primary School, is at RIBA Stage 3 – Detailed Design.The Thompson Primary School is at RIBA Stage 2 – Scheme Design.The projects at Glengormley Integrated Primary School, Kilbride Central Primary School and St Comgall’s Primary School are at RIBA Stage 1 – Technical Feasibility.The SEN projects at Riverside School and Hillcroft School are at RIBA Stage 2 – Scheme Design.Funding continues to be available to progress SEP2 projects through the design stages to pre-tender.  A decision to release projects to tender for a contractor will be taken on a case-by-case basis, dependent on budget availability at that time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether she is aware of any engagement by the regulator OFCOM to investigate the failures by some mobile telecom providers in upgrading networks from 3G and 4G to 5G.
answer_text: Ofcom has advised that it has received very few complaints from customers about the impact of 3G switch off, and operators have not reported to them any significant disruption related to the switch-off. The switch-off will affect customers using older mobile devices and services – most customers already have 4G (and increasingly 5G) capable devices and their services won’t be affected.The British mobile networks are gradually retiring their 2G and 3G networks. All of the mobile providers have confirmed that they do not plan to offer their 2G and 3G services beyond 2033, with two operators having already retired their 3G services. There are no current plans to retire the 4G network, the 4G coverage will continue to provide the backbone of mobile experience for consumers here. As the telecoms sector regulator, Ofcom is responsible for making sure that mobile companies are meeting their obligations to customers. Ofcom has published its expectations from providers 3G and 2G switch-off - Ofcom. Three UK and VMO2 are in the process of switching-off their 3G networks and Ofcom has published further specific VMO2 information Preparing for 2G switch-off - devices using international roaming SIMs: 2G Network Coverage Data and predicted new ‘2G Inbound Roaming Not-Spots’ - Ofcom.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs why the construction of agriculture border inspection posts in Larne, Warrenpoint and Belfast are continuing at a cost to the public purse, when the Welsh Government has paused any further expenditure on identical facilities in Wales.
answer_text: As a result of the Official Controls (Northern Ireland) Regulations 2023, Defra is responsible for the construction of Northern Ireland Sanitary and Phytosanitary Inspection Facilities.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he has had any engagement with Foras na Gaeilge in relation to its funding of the publication Comhar, whose front cover of its July 2025 has been condemned by the Jewish community as being antisemitic.
answer_text: I refer the member to AQW 29755 /22-27

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in light of its funding of the publication Comhar, whose front cover of its July 2025 has been condemned by the Jewish community as being antisemitic, to detail any plans to refer Foras na Gaeilge to be investigated by the Northern Ireland Public Services Ombudsman to ensure that it does not support antisemitism.
answer_text: Public Bodies must ensure that grantees do not breach the conditions of grants and are never in breach of legislation including those related to matters such as libel, slander or hate speech.Foras na Gaeilge received a complaint in relation to the front cover of the July publication of Comhar magazine, which is a third-party organisation that Foras na Gaeilge provides funding to.In line with Foras na Gaeilge’s Service Charter, the complaint was investigated; however, it was not upheld as it applied to Comhar and not Foras na Gaeilge. The complainant was advised to contact Comhar directly.The Northern Ireland Public Services Ombudsman considers complaints from service users and members of the public regarding maladministration by public bodies, once those complaints have exhausted the processes in the public body concerned. The Department therefore has no role in referring Foras na Gaeilge to be investigated by the Ombudsman.I will be raising this issue with Foras na Gaeilge to highlight the concerns that have rightly been expressed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail her Department's costs for the (i) external legal advice and counsel; and (ii) consultancy fees, to date, for (a) the Hightown Incinerator; (b) the Dalradian Curraghinhalt Project; and (c) the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme.
answer_text: My department’s external legal costs in respect of the Hightown Incinerator (Arc21) are £83,520 [Inc VAT], with no expenditure on consultancy.My department’s external legal costs in respect of the Dalradian Curraghinhalt Project are £62,674 and £332,718 [Inc VAT] on consultancy fees. Costs in respect of external legal services in relation to the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme are £405,127 [Ex VAT].  This figure is from 2007 to March 2024. All external legal costs for the 2024/25 and 2025/26 financial years which would include the most recent A5 Western Transport Corridor judicial review proceedings, have not yet been invoiced. The total cost for Client Engineering Managed Services for consultants is £80.6 million [Ex VAT] to March 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, in light of the use of forensic accountants by the Department for Infrastructure in examining the accounts of NI Water, to outline if there are any proposals for similar investigations across other Departments.
answer_text: The use of forensic accountants to examine the accounts of arm's-length bodies is a matter for individual Ministers and Accounting Officers, I would however encourage the use of forensic accountants as a way of seeking reassurance that public funds are being properly utilised and that bids for additional funding are soundly based.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether the services of the forensic accountants, who examined NI Water, was put out to open competition and followed the normal Northern Ireland Civil Service procurement procedures.
answer_text: Given the urgency and sensitivity, a direct award commission was utilised. This was in line with NI Public Procurement Policy and DoF guidance on direct awards, DAO (DoF) 06/22. The value of the contract was below the threshold for application of the Procurement Act 2023.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail whether any business cases have been raised by the Permanent Secretary of the Department for Infrastructure to the Department of Finance for additional funding for legal and consultancy services.
answer_text: The Department for Infrastructure has not submitted any business cases to the Department of Finance for additional funding for legal and consultancy services in this financial year or the previous financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline what proposals his Department is making for cross Northern Ireland Civil Service and arm's-length bodies introduction of Artificial Intelligence.
answer_text: My Department provides a range of shared services, one of which is the provision of common IT infrastructure and applications, such as Microsoft 365. Within the Microsoft 365 suite of products, departments and those arm’s length bodies availing of the IT Assist shared services, have access to Microsoft Copilot, an AI-powered assistant. In addition, my Department is working closely with the Office of AI & Digital, located within The Executive Office, which is working in partnership with executive departments, industry and academia, in developing a roadmap for AI adoption across government departments.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail why his Department does not use the cloud.
answer_text: My Department has a comprehensive Cloud Strategy and makes extensive use of cloud with multiple public cloud providers.  Both Platform as a Service (PaaS) and Software as a Service (SaaS) solutions are employed, together with a cloud-based Data Science and Analytics platform.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) which (a) executive departments; and (b) arm's-length bodies, are not utilising the Cloud; and (ii) the reasons for this.
answer_text: The Department of Finance provides shared IT services to all executive departments and a wide range of arm’s-length bodies, with over 70 separate organisations taking up the IT shared services provided by IT Assist.Many of the standard services provided by IT Assist to all its customers are cloud based services (e.g. email services using Microsoft Exchange Online, collaboration services using Microsoft Teams, amongst others). Therefore, all customers of the IT Assist services provided by my department, including all executive departments, are utilising cloud services.  My Department does not hold information on the use of cloud services by any arm’s-length bodies that do not use the services provided by my department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether any additional support can be provided to sports clubs disrupted by recent violence.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the tonnage of municipal waste collected from each of the local councils involved with arc21, in each of the last five years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the anticipated total costs of Operations (i) Kenova; and (ii) Denton, to date; and (ii) the anticipated eventual total cost.
answer_text: ANSWERI have been advised by PSNI that expenditure on Operation Kenova (including Operation Denton) to 31 March 2025 was £45,877k. A further £340k is projected to be spent this financial year for a total cost of £46,217k.The costs of Operation Denton were not captured separately from wider Operation Kenova costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the difference in the anticipated cost between the overground cable and the high voltage direct current underground cable proposals for the North South Interconnector.
answer_text: I understand that SONI published an Environmental Statement in April 2013 which details the various financial implications associated with constructing the North South Interconnector.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the business case which was made for increasing the staff and budget of the Head of the Northern Ireland Civil Service from 2018-2025.
answer_text: Each additional post was approved in line with TEO and NICS procedures. Business cases for individual posts (where required) were approved as required by TEO’s governance arrangements, which included sign-off by the appropriate senior official.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline the number of schools that have been advised that their School Enhancement Programme has been put on hold by the Education Authority.
answer_text: In recent years, there has been an unprecedented increase in the number of children with Special Educational Needs (SEN).  Since 2019, the total number of children with statements has risen from 19,000 to 27,000 and there has been a 21% rise in pupils attending a special school with many operating at, or beyond, capacity.As part of my Statement to the Assembly of 30 April 2024 announcing the establishment of a SEN Capital Investment Programme, I announced that one of the four strands of the programme would focus on the development and delivery of SEP projects at special schools.  I set out that there are currently ten SEP projects announced in special schools and these projects will be prioritised and expedited for delivery.I can again confirm that this does not mean that the mainstream SEP projects have been paused or placed on hold.  However, at present SEP projects at Special Schools have been prioritised with resource directed to take forward these schemes.Regrettably, this has meant that the programme of mainstream projects has been prolonged, however, the Education Authority (EA) is currently exploring options to increase its resource to take forward SEP projects in mainstream controlled schools as soon as possible.I understand that as a result of this pressure, School Enhancement Programme projects at ten mainstream controlled schools have been advised that there is a delay in the preparation of their business cases, due to EA officers currently prioritising the delivery of additional accommodation for Special Educational Needs placements.I can assure you that there has been no change to the policy regarding SEP.  Funding continues to be available to progress SEP projects through the design stages to pre-tender.  A decision to release projects to tender for a contractor will be taken on a case-by-case basis, dependent on budget availability at that time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline what date in February 2025 she was made aware of any concerns about her Department's Annual Report and Accounts for the year ended 31 March 2024.
answer_text: Question 10 AQO 1819/22-27Initial discussions were held between Department for the Economy officials, the Departmental Audit Committee, and Northern Ireland Audit Office officials in relation to the Comptroller and Auditor General’s disclaimed audit opinions at the beginning of February 2025.More work needed to be completed by NIAO to finalise the details of the disclaimer. When that process concluded NIAO provided the Department with a draft C&AG Audit Certificate, detailing the exact nature of the disclaimed opinion, on the 26 February 2025. Following receipt of the details contained in the draft C&AG Audit Certificate, I was informed of the disclaimed audit opinion by my officials on the 4 March 2025.I would like to make it clear that the concerns raised by the Audit Office through their disclaimed audit opinion do not bring into question issues of value for money, fraud, propriety or budget management.  The disclaimer opinion is in the main, related to the consolidation process and how the Department accounts as a Group for the core department and its multiple Arm’s Length Bodies. These bodies have multiple end-year dates, they work in different currencies, and they operate different accounting standards using different accounting systems, making this consolidation of accounts a hugely complex exercise. I would also like to highlight that each ALB is audited separately, and none of the Department’s ALBs received a disclaimer of opinion on their own Accounts.  I am aware of the issues highlighted by the C&AG regarding Northern Regional College and my officials are working with the college to address those matters.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether any action has been taken against any civil servants in relation to the Comptroller and Auditor General's disclaimed opinion of her Department's Annual Report and Accounts for 2023-24.
answer_text: No action has been taken against Northern Ireland Civil Service staff members in relation to the Comptroller and Auditor General’s (C&AG) disclaimed opinion of her Department’s Annual Report and Accounts for 2023-24 (the Accounts). In her report on the Accounts and the press release following publication, the C&AG highlighted the size and complexity of the Departmental Group from an accounting perspective, noting challenges the Department faces that others do not, such as having 18 component bodies, many with non-coterminous year ends.  The C&AG also highlighted the considerable efforts of Departmental staff in producing the Accounts and facilitating audit procedures.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i), in light of the recommendations of the RHI Inquiry on openness and accountability, to detail whether her Department is in breach of Managing Public Money NI; and (ii) to detail any action she plans to take following the Comptroller and Auditor General's disclaimed opinion of her Department's Annual Report and Accounts for 2023-24.
answer_text: (i) Under paragraph 2.4.5 of Managing Public Money NI (MPMNI) any irregular or improper expenditure must be noted in the Department’s Annual Report and Accounts (the Accounts).  When any expenditure of this kind comes to light, it should be drawn to the attention of both the Northern Ireland Audit Office (NIAO) and the Department of Finance (DoF).In relation to openness and accountability, my Department has complied with MPMNI and has noted irregular expenditure in its Annual Report and Accounts in relation to the Post Graduate Awards grant scheme.  All losses and special payments have also been noted in the Accounts.  All irregular expenditure noted in the Accounts was also notified to DoF.The issues identified by NIAO do not bring into question issues of Value for Money, Propriety or how the Department manages its Budget.  The disclaimer opinion provided by the Comptroller and Auditor General (C&AG) on the Group Accounts is related to the consolidation process and how the Department accounts as a group for the core Department and its Arm’s Length Bodies (ALBs).  It should be noted that each ALB is audited independently and none of the Department’s ALBs received a disclaimer of opinion on their own accounts.(ii) My Department continues to work to make improvements to its processes to produce Group financial statements.  The Department for the Economy (DfE) is committed to ensuring that the deadline for the 2024-25 Accounts is met and my officials have already implemented several improvements in preparation for the 2024-25 Accounts including: further increasing the capacity and capability of the Finance team; increasing support and communications with component bodies; maximising the use of IT solutions to improve communication channels with all key stakeholders throughout the audit process; and enhancing overall review procedures for the consolidation process. A further programme of work, involving working with Partner Organisations and other stakeholders, is already underway.  This involves: finalising a granular level project plan for production of the Accounts; agreeing a detailed plan for the audit with NIAO to work towards laying the 2024/25 Accounts by the statutory date; and carrying out a further review of structures within Finance Division to identify resources to reallocate to this priority area.Given the complexity of the Group Accounts with several components operating with different year ends, on different accounting systems, and using different currencies, it is recognised that resolving all issues is likely to take several years. However, the improvements set out above will make a further step change in the production of the Group Accounts and I expect these proactive actions to deliver further demonstrable improvements to the production of the 2024-25 Group Accounts.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the rationale for not informing the Committee for the Economy nor the Committee for Finance of the Prior Period Adjustments valuing £1.01 billion, as highlighted in her Department's Annual Report and Accounts for 2023-24.
answer_text: ANSWERThe Department of Finance became aware of the audit opinion when it received the Department for the Economy’s 2023-24 Annual Report and Accounts for laying in the Assembly on 5 March 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail when she and her Department were made aware of the Comptroller and Auditor General’s disclaimed opinion on the Department for the Economy’s Annual Report and Accounts 2023-24.
answer_text: Initial discussions were held between Department for the Economy (DfE) officials, the Departmental Audit and Risk Assurance Committee, and Northern Ireland Audit Office (NIAO) officials in relation to the Comptroller and Auditor General’s (C&AG) disclaimed audit opinion at the beginning of February 2025.More work needed to be completed by NIAO to allow the exact details of the disclaimer to be set out.  When that process was concluded NIAO provided the Department with a draft C&AG Audit Certificate, detailing the exact nature of the disclaimed opinion, on the 26 February 2025. Following receipt of the details contained in the draft C&AG Audit Certificate, I was informed of the disclaimed audit opinion on the 4 March 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail when he and his Department were made aware of the Comptroller and Auditor General’s disclaimed opinion on the Department for the Economy’s Annual Report and Accounts 2023-24.
answer_text: ANSWERThe Department of Finance became aware of the audit opinion when it received the Department for the Economy’s 2023-24 Annual Report and Accounts for laying in the Assembly on 5 March 2025.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline any support their Department is providing to refugees from Ukraine.
To ask the Minister of Health to outline his Department's investment into Ballyclare Group Practice.
answer_text: I thank the Member for his ongoing interest. He and I have discussed on a number of occasions the frustrations and concerns being raised locally with him. I fully recognise the need to invest in primary care to support the reform agenda and also to stabilise the Primary Care workforce. Capital investment is required to modernise and maintain that service, and to ensure that the estate is fit for purpose to enable service delivery. Since 2018/19, approximately £38m of capital has been invested through the Primary Care Infrastructure Development programme in GP premises, including investment in accommodation for Primary Care MDT purposes. Ballyclare Group Practice is a large practice with 8 GPs, nursing, allied health and practice support staff and approximately 15,000 patients. Due to the growing size of the Practice, it is now operating out of space which is below its capacity requirements and below the Department of Health recommended schedules of accommodation for a Practice of its size. In February 2024, the Practice submitted an Improvement Grant Application to develop their premises, at George Avenue. The application is for an extension of the existing premises to accommodate Primary Care MDTs. I can confirm that DoH have reviewed the application and have approved in principle. Last month planning approval was granted for the extension by Antrim and Newtownabbey Borough Council. The GP Partners and their professional advisers are currently working with the Infrastructure Development Team and advisers from Health Estates to finalise specifications and design requirements. Given we are at pre-tender stage I can’t yet quantify levels of investment but I can confirm funding has been prioritised to take the extension forward in 2025/26, subject to the finalisation of the Department of Health (draft) Capital Plan and normal business case process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline any role either he or his Department had in relation to the decision to withdraw plans for a national football training centre in Antrim.
answer_text: The Irish FA is leading development of proposals for a National Football Centre. I understand it explored a number of location options before determining a preferred option. The decision on a preferred option is a matter exclusively for the Irish FA. My Department has received a business case which will be considered in line with the usual approvals process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she has reached a decision on the revised planning application on the Hightown incinerator.
answer_text: My officials are continuing to undertake the necessary administrative processes, including assessing consultation advice from the necessary interested bodies and public authorities. In addition, the applicant has also indicated that it intends to submit further information to the Department for consideration. This has yet to be received. When all statutory processes are satisfactorily completed, a recommendation will be brought forward by my officials; however, at this time I cannot confirm when a recommendation will be made. I am keen to bring a resolution to this long-standing application, but if a sound decision is to be reached it is important the planning process is completed correctly
