question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) whether his decision to seek appeal to the Supreme Court on the gas caverns ruling was based on constitutional issues; and (ii) whether he sought cross-cutting approval from the Executive to seek appeal to the Supreme Court on the gas caverns ruling.
answer_text: As indicated in my Written Statement to the Assembly of 9 July 2024, my decision to seek appeal to the Supreme Court in this case reflected my concerns about the constitutional issues raised in the Court of Appeal judgment and the potential implications for decision-making by individual ministers and the threshold for referral of matters to the Executive.  Again as indicated in my Written Statement, I sought the views of all Executive colleagues before reaching my decision.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he was aware that the Irish Football Association was engaged with a private provider on the development of a National Football Training Centre, whilst also in negotiation with Antrim and Newtownabbey Borough Council on the previously agreed site at Birch Hill Road, Antrim.
answer_text: My officials have been working with the Irish FA in relation to The Northern Ireland Football Fund, including the proposal to develop a National Football Centre. I am aware that the Irish FA has been considering a range of options to determine the most appropriate location and specifications for the project. The Irish FA advised me in November 2024 that it no longer intended to pursue purchase of land at Birch Hill in Antrim. This site had not been agreed and no business case to make an application for funding has yet been received by my Department in relation to the National Football Centre. The Irish FA is currently finalising a business case to set out its preferred proposals.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) any actions he is taking to increase spending on the aerospace and defence sector in Northern Ireland by His Majesty’s Government; and (ii) how his Department and Invest NI can help boost that spending, particularly for the Hi-Tech small and medium-sized enterprises sector.
answer_text: Invest NI liaises with external funding providers to identify and leverage alternative funding sources from both British Government and International funders, and boosts the spending of such funds by supporting businesses to apply for external funding across a variety of programmes. In partnership with DfE, DBT and ADS, Invest NI also ensures that businesses in this sector contribute to the development of key British Government strategies. Supplementary to the above, Invest NI supports its client companies (mainly SMEs) to showcase their supply chain capabilities at key international trade events.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the potential impact that the new EU-Mercosur agreement will have on the agricultural sector in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: While an agreed free trade agreement between the EU and Mercosur was announced on 6th December 2024 it is likely to be some time before this agreement is fully ratified and comes into effect. Talks in respect of an EU-Mercosur free trade agreement first commenced in 1999 with an agreement in principle being reached some ten years later in June 2019. It is expected that progress on the full implementation of this agreement will remain slow.While the agreement does not apply to the UK, it does have the potential to impact UK agricultural markets including Northern Ireland, due to increased imports from Mercosur into the EU, particularly beef and poultry. I appreciate the concerns expressed by the agricultural sector in relation to this agreement but imports under reduced tariffs will be limited by quota for some products including beef where the concessionary tariff of 7.5% is limited to 99,000 tonnes and poultry where tariff free access is limited to 180,000 tonnes.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he has had communication with the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs or the Minister for the Economy on incineration for power projects.

In the most recent communication received by my officials, the agent for this application has indicated a submission containing information to address some of the issues raised in recent consultation responses shall be submitted imminently.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance (i) whether any performance-related pay has been made to the Head of the Civil Service and members of the Northern Ireland Civil Service Board; and (ii), in light of the Northern Ireland Audit Office's second report on implementation of RHI Inquiry recommendations, to detail the criteria for any payments being made.
answer_text: The Chartered Institute of Personnel and Development (CIPD) defines performance-related pay as a way of managing pay, achieved by linking salary progression to an assessment of individual performance. Civil servants here move up each pay-point in the pay scale for their grade each year (with effect from the pay award date, until they reach the top of the scale) unless they receive an unsatisfactory performance appraisal assessment.  The performance management system applies to all civil servants, including the Senior Civil Service.  There are seven increments in the pay scales for Grade 2 (Permanent Secretaries) and Head of Civil Service.  There are no other performance-related pay or bonuses in operation in the Civil Service (apart from a productivity bonus scheme for roadworkers in Department for Infrastructure).

answer_text: There are nine schools progressing under the School Enhancement Programme (SEP) in South Antrim. question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) to detail the aviation and airport management experience within his Department; and (ii) whether he is considering employing additional staff to manage the City of Derry Airport.
answer_text: My Department is responsible for the development and implementation of air connectivity and, from 1 April 2025, airport policy. As civil aviation is a reserved matter my officials work closely with the Department for Transport to maintain and enhance air routes which are vital to our economy.  We are also working closely with all three of our local airports to consider potential policy levers and opportunities for route development. I am not considering employing additional staff to manage the City of Derry Airport where there is an established management team already working closely with Derry City & Strabane District Council.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the financial position facing his Department.
answer_text: The Department of Health is facing significant financial challenges not just for 2024-25 but in future years.While the £122m received in June monitoring made some contribution to reducing the deficit position, a shortfall of £143m still remained to cover existing spending.With an additional push for savings from our ALBs and an assumption that we will receive an additional £130m through in-year monitoring I hope this gap can be bridged.However, this still means that there is currently no budgetary provision for 2024-25 pay settlements to staff, the cost of which is currently estimated to be in the region of £300m.The level of savings measures required to fund a pay award in 2024-25 would require the implementation of some measures that will be contrary to the interests of health and social care, requiring us to actively shrink the HSC and resulting in more pressures on services and staff and yet more gaps in provision at a time when the clamour is understandably to invest in more doctors, nurses and other health professionals. Our expectation for 2025-26 is that the funding position will not significantly improve for Health, further reductions to services may have to be considered and it will be extremely challenging to secure funding for new activity.  Therefore, we will need to focus on making best use of the resources we already have through greater levels of productivity.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail his plans to ensure the security and continuity of electricity supply in light of recent legal judgements on Kilroot power station.
answer_text: Energy security is a key priority for the Department.  The Energy Strategy ‘A Pathway to Net Zero’ sets out my department’s ambition to deliver an energy system that is secure, affordable and sustainable. Security of supply for electricity is a risk managed collaboratively by government, the Utility Regulator and SONI (the Electricity Transmission System Operator).My officials work on an ongoing basis with the energy industry, both electricity and gas, and the Utility Regulator to review and monitor short, medium and long-term security of supply and ensure our changing energy system is robust and resilient.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he will commission a trade dashboard to detail the implications of the Windsor Framework.
answer_text: This is the responsibility of HMRC who collect and publish information apportioning international trade between Britain and the North.	Additionally, NISRA is responsible for producing the Northern Ireland Economic Trade Statistics (NIETS). NIETS provides an official annual measure of local businesses’ domestic, regional and international sales and purchases with markets outside the North.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she will commission an independent investigation into the operation of the voluntary exit scheme and its provisions in respect to the Northern Ireland Prison Service.
answer_text: There are no plans to revisit the operation of the Voluntary Early Retirement Scheme which closed in May 2014.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any proposals his Department has to support additional interconnection between Northern Ireland and Great Britain.
answer_text: Transmission Investment has proposed a new interconnector (also known as LiRIC), between the north of Ireland and Scotland, subject to approval by the Utility Regulator and Ofgem. The Utility Regulator is currently working on Transmission Investments’ license application. Whilst my Department is working closely with the Utility Regulator on interconnection, it has no role in the license approval process and must remain impartial.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) whether she has made a comparison of the rate of sickness in the Northern Ireland Prison Service compared to the rest of the Northern Ireland Civil Service; and (ii) whether she has made an assessment of the amount of occupational health support to prison staff.
answer_text: I am concerned by the level of sickness absence within the Northern Ireland Prison Service (NIPS) and, indeed, in my Department and across the Northern Ireland Civil Service.  Reducing the level of sickness absence remains a priority and my officials are engaging with colleagues in NICS HR within the Department of Finance, to consider the most recent published statistics and potential management actions.  It is important, however, that there is an understanding and supportive approach in the management of sickness absence while also acknowledging the significant impact of ill-health and sickness absence on the cost and quality of service delivery.  Staff within NIPS experience the same types of illnesses as the wider population; however, due to the frontline operational nature of their business, they also face unique challenges not experienced by other parts of the NICS.  This can impact on levels of sickness absence.  For example, within the latest reports published by the Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency, Prison (operational) Grade staff had a noticeably higher proportion of working days lost to Injury/Fracture than staff in other grade levels.  All sickness absences within NIPS are managed in line with the relevant NICS sickness absence policies.  The aim of those policies is to promote regular attendance at work, minimise the level of absence, and to provide a framework within which sickness absence can be effectively managed.   There will continue to be a focus on the management of sickness absence across NIPS through HR processes as well as promoting health and well-being initiatives.  Prison Officers have access to the NICS Welfare Support Services and the NICS Occupational Health Service.  They can also avail of Inspire Workplace Services and the NICS confidential Employee Assistance Programme (EAP) which provides bespoke counselling services.  In addition to these services, NIPS provides additional support for Prison Officers through the Police Rehabilitation and Retraining Trust (PRRT).  NIPS’ partnership with PRRT continues to evolve and during 2023 over 400 operational staff received support through PRRT, including psychology and physio services; resilience training; and a Health and Wellness Programme developed specifically for prison staff.  Prison Officers can also seek support through the NIPS Critical Incident Response Stress Management (CISM) initiative, which provides a confidential and voluntary debriefing procedure to help normalise responses to minor, moderate and severe critical incidents.   Finally, NICS HR within the Department of Finance is currently developing a Health and Wellbeing Strategy which will include mental health as a key component.  This is in the final stages of development and will be launched in October.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline his plans in relation to the Hightown incinerator project, in light of opposition from local residents and Antrim and Newtownabbey Borough Council.
answer_text: I am aware of the number of responses to the planning application and the opposition that exists both locally and amongst some colleagues. My Department in considering all representations to any application will give proper consideration to their content  where they raise material planning issues. My officials will therefore continue to process this application and then, as Minister with responsibility for this planning decision, I will fully consider the recommendation that comes from them before taking a decision in the public interest.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the future provision of North South and East West electricity interconnectors.
answer_text: Electricity interconnectors are an important tool in delivering the Energy Strategy and the Climate Change Act targets. My department is working with SONI and NIE Networks on delivery of the second North South Interconnector and completion is expected in 2028. SONI is working to optimise the Moyle Interconnector’s import capacity by upgrading the network. Transmission Investment has proposed a new interconnector (also known as LiRIC), between the North and Scotland, subject to approval by the Utility Regulator and Ofgem.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline his proposals to support the aerospace and defence sectors.
answer_text: Question 11 - AQO 650/22-27With over 100 companies and dominated by SMEs, the Aerospace Sector employs almost 8,000 workers and contributes an estimated £1.9bn to the local economy. It’s supply chain is award winning and globally recognised.My Department and Invest NI support the sector through Investment in growth, R&D, Training, and exports. We also help the sector look at emerging technologies including Space, Urban Air Mobility and Future Flight. In the period 2020 – 2023, Invest NI provided 142 offers of support from a range of schemes totalling £13.81m.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he plans to ban electric and prong collars for use with cats and canines under the Welfare of Animals Act (Northern Ireland) 2011.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of whether the temporary suspension of granting permission to build new waste incinerators in England should apply in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I note that the Defra Minister, Mark Spencer MP, issued a direction to the Environment Agency to pause determination of waste environmental incineration permits from 5 April 2024 to 24 May 2024 to scope a review of infrastructure needs. The direction applies to England only.In Northern Ireland, the Department for Infrastructure and local councils have responsibility for granting planning permissions. My Department’s role is limited to commenting on individual planning applications as a statutory consultee if requested. Therefore any decision temporarily to suspend the granting of permissions to build new waste incinerators does not rest with my department.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the ongoing analysis by the Strategic Investment Board on residual waste treatment capacity in Northern Ireland, including when such assessments may be both concluded and published.
answer_text: There is no ongoing analysis of residual waste treatment capacity in Northern Ireland, however, I aim to commission a new analysis by the Strategic Investment Board in line with the Climate Change Committee recommendation.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what discussions he has had with the Home Office in relation to the implementation of the Electronic Travel Authorisation scheme.
answer_text: I refer to my Answer to AQW 10297/22-27 on 30 April 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether the RHI Inquiry recommendations in relation to the recruitment process of senior civil servants to senior posts in the civil service and arm's-length bodies have been implemented.
answer_text: The Inquiry recommendations in respect of recruitment only related to the civil service.An update on the implementation of the recommendations of the RHI Inquiry can be viewed here.With regard to senior civil service (‘SCS’) recruitment, in the NICS, these roles are recruited through job role specific competitions with eligibility criteria aligned to the particular skills and experience required to effectively deliver the job role.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether there are plans to move the gullies on Tildarg Road, Ballyclare, following drainage issues identified by local residents.
answer_text: My Department has recently received a request to relocate a gully on Tildarg Road.  This is currently being considered by the local Section Office.  However, if any residents are experiencing flooding, or any other road related issues, they should raise this through the online reporting tool, using the link below in order that appropriate action can be taken:https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/information-and-services/travel-transport-and-roads/problems-roads-and-streets

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what plans his Department has to improve street lighting at the Ahoghill Road bus station, adjacent to Coolsythe Road, Randalstown, following safety issues being raised by local residents.
answer_text: My Department’s policy for the provision of street lighting in rural locations considers:-The density of housing within a community, including public buildings with significant nighttime use; and, Road safety, where street lighting would contribute to a reduction in the number of night-time collisions.These criteria serve to balance the demand for more rural lighting against the unwelcome effects of increased urbanisation of the countryside, the environmental impact of night sky light pollution on people, wildlife and flora and the financial costs of providing and maintaining additional public lighting installations.My officials have assessed this location and have confirmed that it does not fulfil the above criteria. Consequently, there are no plans to install street lighting.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline his proposals to replace the Non-domestic Renewable Heat Incentive scheme.
answer_text: Oral Question No 9 – AQO 361/22-27New Decade New Approach states that “RHI will be closed down and replaced by a scheme that effectively cuts carbon emissions.” I have a paper with the Executive seeking agreement to honour this commitment. This agreement will allow negotiations with Treasury to proceed on how the AME funding could be used more effectively in the future. In the interim, my Department is working in partnership with Invest NI to deliver financial support for businesses through a new scaled phase of its Energy & Resource Efficiency (ERE) Programme. The Programme will include a new Energy Efficiency Capital Grant Scheme (EECG) which will be open to the wider business base and is due to launch in the coming weeks. The scheme will focus on capital grant support for energy efficiency measures along with low carbon and renewable technologies, all aimed at reducing emissions and helping businesses take control of their energy use by lowering their energy demand. The wider aspects of the programme include support in the form of Technical Consultancy, Resource Matching Through Industrial Symbiosis and a Resource Efficiency Capital Grant which are already in operation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the financial cost to date of Operation Kenova; and (ii) an estimated final financial cost of Operation Kenova.
answer_text: Operation Kenova was set up by the PSNI and, as such was not under the purview of the Department of Justice, as it was an operational matter. The information requested would be held by the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the final financial cost of the Ballymurphy Inquest.
answer_text: The majority of legacy inquest costs (e.g. staffing costs and overheads) relate to the overall costs of legacy inquest units within relevant criminal justice organisations and, therefore, cannot be allocated to specific inquests.  For this reason, the total costs of individual legacy inquests cannot be specified.  It is, however, possible to provide details of costs that are specific to individual cases. The Ballymurphy inquests, five in total, looked into the deaths of 10 people in Belfast in 1971. The inquests commenced in November 2018 and the Coroner’s findings were delivered in May 2021. Details of the final financial costs (since the Legacy Inquest Project commenced on 1 April 2019), broken down by category, are provided below.  Please note, since reply provided in AQW 2977517-22, further costs totalling £137k have been paid by the Legal Services Agency (LSA).  However, in August 2022, LSA received £1,533k of costs recovered in respect of the inquests as part of the settlement of related civil actions.  This means LSA’s overall net legal aid costs of the inquest stands at £199k.  This is reflected in the below summary of costs and has resulted in a reduction to the final financial costs of the inquests.Summary of costs for Ballymurphy Inquests:Category£kSolicitors’ Costs74Counsel Fees and Legal Costs2,597Disbursements15Forensic Costs76Professional/Expert Witness Expenses63Stenographer Costs63Witness Expenses60Delivery of Findings and Associated Costs24Exhumations4Total2,976

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) what discussions he has had with the Home Office over the forthcoming introduction of the Electronic Travel Authorisation (ETA); and (ii) whether his officials have made an assessment of the impact the ETA will have on tourism spend.
answer_text: I am concerned about the impact of the introduction of the British Government’s Electronic Travel Authorisation (ETA) Scheme on our tourism sector. I see it as a real risk to the huge growth potential for tourism here. I have met with the NI Tourism Alliance to discuss this vital issue and to hear at first hand the industry’s concerns. I have written to Tom Pursglove MP, the Minister for Legal Migration and the Border at the British Home Office to seek an urgent meeting to discuss solutions that would protect our tourism industry.With the British Government’s intentions to roll-out their ETA Scheme in full by early 2025, my officials alongside those in both Tourism Ireland and Tourism NI are engaged with the British Home Office in its development and roll-out of a targeted and bespoke communication strategy, particular to island of Ireland travel. Communication on the scheme needs to be delivered appropriately in a bid to minimise visitors being discouraged from visiting either here or potentially the island of Ireland as a whole.My preference however is that the British Government provides an exemption from the ETA for tourists visiting the North.In 2019, international visitors (who will require an ETA when introduced) contributed £220 million to the local economy and it is this expenditure that could be impacted by the introduction of the ETA scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the financial cost to date of the Coagh Inquests; and (ii) an estimated final financial cost of the Coagh Inquests.
answer_text: The majority of legacy inquest costs (e.g. staffing costs and overheads) relate to the overall costs of legacy inquest units within relevant criminal justice organisations and, therefore, cannot be allocated to specific inquests.  For this reason, the total costs of individual legacy inquests cannot be specified.  It is, however, possible to provide details of costs that are specific to individual cases. Furthermore, the final financial cost of an inquest will not be known until all final bills have been submitted and assessed.  It is, therefore, difficult to estimate the final financial costs of the Coagh Inquest until this process has been completed. The Coagh Inquest investigated the deaths of three people, on 3 June 1991, in the village of Coagh, County Tyrone.  The inquest commenced in September 2022 with the Coroner’s findings delivered on 11 April 2024. Details of both the financial costs to date (since the Legacy Inquest Project commenced on 1 April 2019) and estimated final financial costs for the Coagh inquest are provided below, broken down by category:Summary of costs for Coagh Inquest:Category(i) Financial costs to date£k(ii)Estimated final financial costs£kCounsel Fees and Legal Costs367380Solicitors’ Costs5353Disbursements3939Professional/Expert Witness Costs264264Stenographer Costs2020Outlays254254Total9971,010

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline what proposals he has for mitigating the impact of the Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism on the economy.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what action she is taking to address the ongoing human resource issues within the NI Prison Service.
To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what progress has been made in addressing rising nitrate levels in Lough Neagh and the watercourses feeding into it.
answer_text: I intend to bring the Lough Neagh Report to the Executive in the very near future which will set out recommendations to target high nutrient levels, a major cause of problems in the Lough.  Action on the ground has already commenced with the College for Agriculture, Food and Rural Enterprise having delivered nutrient management programmes for farmers/land managers at various levels targeted towards the Lough Neagh catchment areas.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice when she proposes that the Police Service of Northern Ireland will achieve pay parity with their colleagues in England and Wales, outside of the Metropolitan Police.
answer_text: The Department supports the principle of broad alignment on the main pay and allowances for the PSNI with their counterparts in England and Wales. Indeed, each year the Department’s remit letter to the Police Remuneration Review Body (PRRB) asks that their considerations take account of this principle of broad alignment. The core pay scales for PSNI officers up to and including the rank of Assistant Chief Constable align with their counterparts in England and Wales.  However, it is not possible to have parity across every aspect of pay and allowances. For example, PSNI officers receive Northern Ireland Transitional Allowance (NITA) which acknowledges the impact of the security threat on their private lives and that of their families.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what plans he has to deal with road safety on (i) Ballycorr Road; and (ii) Henryville Manor, Ballyclare.
answer_text: I have requested that an official from the Ballymena Traffic Management Office will contact you to discuss your specific concerns for each area. Once these specific concerns are established, officials will investigate what, if any, solutions are available.One of the best ways to ensure safety on our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury by 2050, is by changing road user behaviour. As road users we all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe. The sad reality is that if we do not change our own attitudes when we are using the roads, our death toll will continue to increase.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) whether he has noted the ministerial direction to temporarily suspend future domestic waste incineration proposals in England and the intention to reduce waste incineration by 50 per cent; and (ii) whether he has instructed his officials to undertake a similar review.
answer_text: I note that Defra Minister Mark Spencer issued a direction to the Environment Agency to pause determination of waste environmental incineration permits from 5 April 2024 to 24 May 2024 to scope a review of infrastructure needs. The direction applies to England only.There are no plans to undertake a similar review in Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline the measures they have in place to ensure that appropriate scrutiny of all EU legislation and regulations can be carried out in a timely manner.
answer_text: The EU notifies the UK, including Northern Ireland, of any new EU laws that amend or replace the legislation listed in the annexes of the Windsor Framework, as well as new laws where the EU intends to request their addition.  Notifications are disseminated by TEO to policy leads across NICS departments using the EU Legislation Information Tracking System (EULITS) system.The Brussels Office monitors the development of relevant legislation though the legislative progress and provides updates to departmental policy leads.  They also carry out upstream monitoring using information such as the Commission Work Programme.In addition to monitoring the formal processes, the Brussels office also informally engages with representatives of EU Institutions and other stakeholders to gather information to assist policy leads in their understanding of issues.  Throughout the process departmental policy leads can ask the EU for clarification via the Joint Consultative Working Group and its structured subgroups.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail their plans to table an applicability motion on Regulation (EU) 2023/2411 on geographical indication protection for craft and industrial products within the time period set out in legislation.
answer_text: We have no plans to jointly table an Applicability Motion specific to Regulation (EU) 2023/2411, covering Non-Agricultural Geographic Indicators.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they have directed the Head of the Civil Service to carry out an audit of the legislative remits of each department to identify matters that might fall to more than one department.
answer_text: We have no plans to commission such an audit.  Statutory responsibility for functions is usually attributed to only one department and any uncertainty concerning departmental responsibility can be resolved on a case by case basis.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline his proposals to promote the aerospace and defence sector.
answer_text: There are over 100 companies in our aerospace sector which employ almost 9,000 people. The sector contributes an estimated £2.2bn to the local economy, based on figures from ADS, the national trade body for Aerospace and Defence. My department and Invest NI help promote the sector through support towards investment, R&D, training, and international trade. For example, in July Invest NI hosted 11 companies at the Farnborough Airshow, helping them build important business opportunities. We also help the sector look at future emerging technologies including Space, Urban Air Mobility and Future Flight.
