question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) whether he is aware of the poor condition of (a) Ballycorr Road; and (b) Thornhill Road, South Antrim; and (ii) whether he will prioritise some of the £1 million pothole repair budget on these roads.
answer_text: While there are many roads that would benefit from investment, including the Ballycorr Road and Thornhill Road, due to limited budget resources, resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need for intervention. I am very aware of the long-term underfunding from which our road network has suffered.Given the on-going work associated with new housing developments adjacent to the Ballycorr Road, which is likely to give rise to additional construction traffic, utility works and the further potential for a right turn lane on the Ballycorr Road, it would not be appropriate to invest significant funding in resurfacing at this time. Once this work is complete, further consideration can be given to including the Ballycorr Road in the future resurfacing programme. In the meantime, my Department will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with the current maintenance policy.I can confirm that Thornhill Road is included in the Department’s surface dressing programme for 2024/25 subject to the availability of funding. The importance of a well maintained road network cannot be understated but this requires investment. I will continue to seek the support of Executive colleagues to enable this to happen.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) to provide an update on Antrim and Newtownabbey's Local Development Plan; and (ii) when this update will be made available to stakeholders.
answer_text: The Department received the Independent Examination report on Antrim and Newtownabbey Borough Council draft Plan Strategy (PS) on 4 October 2023.  The report is currently under consideration by officials.  DfI will engage directly in due course with the Council as part of standard procedure.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, in light of the expanding development in housing in the Ballyclare and Doagh areas, whether the school development programmes announced by his predecessor are being continued.
answer_text: Ballyclare High School and Ballyclare Secondary School are two of twenty-eight schools announced to advance in planning in March 2022.  All twenty-eight projects were subsequently paused in April 2023, due to the capital budget position.  On 12 February 2024 I advised that seven of the projects from the March 2022 announcement would now progress in planning, however Ballyclare High School and Ballyclare Secondary School were not included in the seven projects and thus these projects remain paused.Without additional capital funding these projects will remain paused.  As I have referenced on a number of occasions, I will be bidding for additional capital funding to deliver much needed capital projects.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they have sufficient staff to effectively horizon scan for future EU legislation, particularly for applicability motions that are outside the remit of the Windsor Framework Democratic Scrutiny Committee.
answer_text: It is for each department to determine the level of resources they require to effectively horizon scan for future EU legislation. In addition, the Executive Office provides some assistance to departments in this respect, including through the Office of the Northern Ireland Executive in Brussels, and our engagement with UK Government and EU officials as appropriate. The Executive Office also convenes cross-Departmental fora to pool intelligence on forthcoming EU legislation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) what progress has been made on the removal of the maximum student number cap; and (ii) what proposals he is seeking in overall numbers.
answer_text: I have already met with Queen’s University Belfast and Ulster University for wide-ranging discussions which included consideration of the impact of the maximum student number cap.  I intend to develop proposals on this issue which will help our universities to grow in a financially sustainable and responsible way.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) what reassurances has he received on the progress of North-South and East-West Interconnectors; and (ii) whether delays in planning will impact on energy security.
answer_text: With regard to the North-South interconnector, on 14 September 2020, the Department of Infrastructure granted planning approval for (i) the erection of a 400Kv overhead electric line and (ii) to carry out works associated with the construction. With regard to a proposed East-West interconnector, known as LirlC, the company involved, Transmission Investment, has applied for a Transmission Licence, which is currently under consideration by the Utility Regulator and SONI (the System Operator for NI).My Department supports the use of interconnectors to improve security of supply and potentially reduce prices for consumers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) whether any studies have been undertaken to installing a rail halt at Ballymartin, Co. Antrim; and (ii) whether this can be considered as part of any future strategic rail plan.
answer_text: Translink is undertaking a feasibility study on the potential for enhancing capacity and journey times on the existing Belfast – Derry rail corridor. This study will inform a Strategic Outline Case which will consider the demand and operational impact of introducing any additional halts at a number of locations, including Ballymartin. The Strategic Outline Case is due to be submitted to the Department in Quarter 2 of 2024.  My Department is working towards finalising the All-Island Strategic Rail Review which considers how the rail network on the island of Ireland can improve sustainable connectivity between major cities, enhance regional accessibility, support balanced regional development and contribute to decarbonisation. The Review includes a recommendation to explore the case for developing new stations in the Belfast and Derry regions. However, being at a strategic level, the Review has not considered the location of these stations. Once the Review is published, further work will be required to prioritise the recommendations and then to undertake feasibility studies and develop business cases to support any future investment decisions, including those in relation to the development of new rail halts along the existing network.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline the reporting requirements of the Inter-governmental and International Relations Directorate to the Windsor Framework Democratic Scrutiny Committee.
answer_text: There are no statutory reporting requirements for The Executive Office relating to the Windsor Framework Democratic Scrutiny Committee. TEO officials are, however, active in assisting the Democratic Scrutiny Committee in its work. For example, TEO is the single point of contact for the Democratic Scrutiny Committee when seeking information from one or more Executive departments relating to new or replacement EU legislation within the scope of the Windsor Framework.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline the status of the consultations on measures to support budget sustainability and raise additional revenue.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he has instructed his permanent secretary to desist from seeking to challenge NoArc21 in its campaign against the Hightown Incinerator.
answer_text: On 31 May 2023 the High Court quashed the decision to refuse planning permission for the waste treatment facility (planning application ref: T/2014/0114/F) and remitted the application back to my department for determination. Any individual or group can continue to make representations in respect of the proposed development during the planning application process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she will consider commencing an independent investigation into the human resources and mental health support provided to Prison Service staff.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Prison Service (NIPS) is an agency of the Department of Justice and therefore is part of the wider civil service, with HR services delivered centrally through NICSHR, in the Department of Finance (DoF).In the previous mandate, I commissioned an independent review of the support services for serving prison officers which included the provision of HR services and mental health support provided to Prison Service staff.  Following that review, in June 2021, in partnership with the DoF, NIPS established an embedded HR function where dedicated NICSHR staff are situated on site across each of the prison establishments (Maghaberry, Hydebank and Magilligan) and Prison Service Headquarters.  This arrangement is bespoke to NIPS and these DoF staff provide dedicated HR Business Partnering services and advice to Prison Officers and other staff working within NIPS.  In addition, I am advised a review of the Occupational Health Service (OHS), a division within NICSHR, was completed in June 2023 by DoF with input from all NICS departments.  The programme of transformation for OHS has already commenced, including developing a multidisciplinary occupational health and wellbeing advice service for the whole of the NICS including Prison Officers.  Some work has already commenced to provide specific services for prison officers, such as vaccinations on site within the prison estate.  NIPS partnership with the Police Retraining and Rehabilitation Trust (PRRT) continues to evolve.  During 2023 over 400 operational staff received a range of services through PRRT, including psychology and physiotherapy services; resilience training; and a Health and Wellness Programme developed specifically for prison staff. Prison Officers can also access the full range of health and wellbeing services available to NICS staff through NICSHR.  These include the Welfare Support Service, the Well Programme, an Employee Assistance Programme, a self-referral facility to confidential counselling through Inspire Workplace Services plus a range of learning and development interventions that staff can access.Therefore, for the reasons set out above I have no plans to commence an independent investigation as referred.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether she will conduct an independent investigation into the human resources and mental health support provided by NICS HR to Prison Service staff.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Prison Service (NIPS) is an agency of the Department of Justice and therefore is part of the wider civil service, with HR services delivered centrally through NICSHR, in the Department of Finance (DoF).In the previous mandate, the Justice Minister Naomi Long MLA commissioned an independent review of the support services for serving prison officers which included the provision of HR services and mental health support provided to Prison Service staff. Following that review, in June 2021, in partnership with the DoF, NIPS established an embedded HR function where dedicated NICSHR staff are situated on site across each of the prison establishments (Maghaberry, Hydebank and Magilligan) and Prison Service Headquarters. This arrangement is bespoke to the NIPS and these DoF staff provide dedicated HR Business Partnering services and advice to Prison Officers and other staff working within the NIPS.  A review of the OHS, a division within NICSHR, was completed in June 2023 by DoF with input from all NICS departments. The programme of transformation for OHS has already commenced, including developing a multidisciplinary occupational health and wellbeing advice service for the whole of the NICS including Prison Officers.  Some work has already commenced to provide specific services for prison officers, such as vaccinations on site within the prison estate.I am advised that the NIPS has a separate arrangement with the Police Rehabilitation and Retraining Trust (PRRT) for psychological and physiotherapy treatments and the referrals for same are managed by the embedded HR team. Prison Officers can also access the full range of health and wellbeing services available to NICS staff through NICSHR.  These include the Welfare Support Service, the Well Programme, an Employee Assistance Programme, the self-referral facility to confidential counselling through Inspire Workplace Services and a range of learning and development interventions that staff can access as well as access to Occupation Health Services.  In addition to this, the NICS provides part grant funding to the Charity for Civil Servants which provides a range of support to staff including advising individuals on financial matters, general health and wellbeing, or for staff with caring responsibilities.Therefore, for the reasons set out above I have no plans to commence an independent investigation as referred.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline the mechanisms they have in place to monitor all EU regulations.
answer_text: The Executive Office has put in place a number of structures to horizon scan and monitor current and future EU Regulations.  These include monitoring the EU legislative process at its various stages and engaging with UK Government and EU officials as appropriate. Executive Office officials regularly attend the Joint Consultative Working Group (JCWG) meetings with the EU as part of the UK delegation.  This group serves as a joint EU – UK forum for the exchange of information and mutual consultation on the implementation of the Windsor Framework.Information received from the EU via this group is disseminated to NICS policy leads by the Executive Office using the bespoke EU Legislation Information Tracking System (EULITS).Executive Office officials have also established a Cross-Departmental Working Group on Managing Divergence, and a Cross-Departmental Steering Group on Europe which has a standing item on ‘Foresight and Horizon Scanning’ of EU Policy and Legislation.It should be noted that these groups pre-dated the new Command Paper.This work is supported by an NICS wide Capability Action Plan to enhance the capability of civil servants to identify the impacts of divergence on policy at the earliest opportunity.Additionally, our office in Brussels monitors the development of EU legislation within the scope of the Windsor Framework, and tracks progress of relevant legislation from the consultation phase to the legislative process.  The Office also informally engages with representatives of EU Institutions and other stakeholders to gather pertinent information.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission whether the Assembly Library will compile, publish and regularly update a list of the EU regulations made since the introduction of the Protocol that pertain to Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Assembly Library itself has no plans to compile, publish and regularly update a list of the EU regulations made since the introduction of the Protocol that pertain to Northern Ireland, given the existence of dedicated European Commission resources and the potential future availability of UK Government or Northern Ireland Executive Resources.  The Research and Information Service’s EU Exit Hub contains a range of information relating to the UK’s exit from the European Union, with particular reference to Northern Ireland. The Protocol on Ireland/Northern Ireland area of the hub introduces and provides a link to the dedicated EUR-Lex resources covering relevant EU legislation in respect of Northern Ireland. EUR-Lex, which is run by the Publications Office of the European Union, provides the official and most comprehensive access to EU legal documents and is updated daily. The link can be found by navigating to the page on ‘Dynamic Alignment’ within the EU Exit Hub.The EUR-Lex resources referred to are operated unilaterally by the European Commission, and their contents have not been endorsed by the UK Government. The potential development and sharing of a ‘UK Government log of regulatory divergence between Northern Ireland and Great Britain arising from EU and domestic legislation’ is the subject of ongoing correspondence between Lord Jay of Ewelme, Chair of the House of Lords European Affairs Sub-Committee on the Protocol on Ireland/Northern Ireland, and the Rt Hon James Cleverly MP, Secretary of State for Foreign, Commonwealth and Development Affairs.In January 2022, in a departmental briefing note provided to the Assembly’s Committee for the Executive Office, it was stated that the development of an EU Legislation Information Tracking System (EULITS) to track and monitor changes to legislation listed in the annexes of the Protocol was ongoing.The briefing note stated that the EULITS would assist the Executive Office to transmit details of proposed and adopted EU legislative measures to the relevant departments which have policy competence and responsibility forcomplying with the legislation.Given the existence of the dedicated EUR-Lex resource and the potential future availability of UK Government or Northern Ireland Executive resources, the Library has no plans itself to compile or publish a list of the EU regulations made since the introduction of the Protocol that pertain to Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the ongoing divergence issues related to medicines and the Northern Ireland Protocol.
answer_text: My Department has been consistent in seeking to preserve equity of access to medicines for the people of Northern Ireland in line with the rest of the United Kingdom. To secure prescription-only medicines supply to Northern Ireland the Northern Ireland MHRA Authorised Route (NIMAR) was implemented in January 2022.  NIMAR has allowed branded prescription-only medicines authorised for Great Britain to be supplied into Northern Ireland if there is clinical need and no licenced alternatives are available. In April 2022, the European Commission provided legislation on medicines and the Northern Ireland Protocol which addressed some of the issues raised by my Department to the United Kingdom government. However, there remain issues associated with the implementation of the Northern Ireland Protocol for medicines that risk divergence between Northern Ireland and Great Britain.  Northern Ireland continues to comply with the European Union acquis in respect of medicines and medical devices while Great Britain does not.  Therefore, unlike Great Britain, Northern Ireland must comply with the Falsified Medicines Directive and rely upon European Medicines Agency (EMA) authorisations for Centrally Authorised Products which include vaccines and innovative medicines.In relation to Centrally Authorised Products, differences in regulatory approval timelines and authorisation conditions between Northern Ireland and Great Britain are increasing in frequency and complexity.  This is placing at risk my Department’s long standing policy link with the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE), for the managed entry of new medicines in Northern Ireland. NICE recommendations are based on the Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA) authorisations for novel medicines.  Any divergence between MHRA and EMA authorisations means that only partial endorsement of NICE guidance is possible in Northern Ireland in line with the EMA licence.  I have raised these issues with the Department of Health and Social Care (DHSC) and my officials will continue to work closely with DHSC as there is the immediate need for a solution for those issues not addressed by the April 2022, European Commission legislation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the use of wayleaves to progress the construction of the North South Interconnector, where disputes between SONI and landowners have not been resolved.
answer_text: Under Electricity (Northern Ireland) Order 1992, SONI may apply for the grant of a necessary wayleave. My Department is under a statutory duty to consider and determine any such application. Before making any decision, my Department will afford the landowner affected an opportunity to be heard. My Department has not discussed necessary wayleaves for the North South Interconnector with the Utility Regulator.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he has had discussions with the Utility Regulator in relation to the use of wayleaves to progress the construction of the North South Interconnector where disputes between SONI and landowners have not been resolved.
answer_text: Under Electricity (Northern Ireland) Order 1992, SONI may apply for the grant of a necessary wayleave. My Department is under a statutory duty to consider and determine any such application. Before making any decision, my Department will afford the landowner affected an opportunity to be heard. My Department has not discussed necessary wayleaves for the North South Interconnector with the Utility Regulator.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in light of recent reports that Northern Ireland has a secure and diverse energy supply, whether he will publish representations that have been made by SONI on the continuing rationale of the North-South Interconnector project.
answer_text: Planning Permission for the North South Interconnector was granted by the Department on 14 September 2020.  As such the implementation of the project is now entirely a matter for the developer SONI and any representations now made by SONI in respect of the rationale of the project are not submitted to the Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, further to Ofgem’s letter to the BSC Panel Chair, dated 30 September 2022, stating that there is a significant risk of gas shortages during winter 2022-23, what discussions he has had with the Northern Ireland Utility Regulator and electricity suppliers to ensure there will be no disruption to Northern Ireland’s electricity supply.
answer_text: My Department is in regular contact with BEIS regarding the security of gas supply to Northern Ireland. We meet with the Utility Regulator and NI gas and electricity system operators on a regular basis to ensure that the latest position regarding gas supply is relayed to industry. DfE also continues to liaise with gas and electricity industry to ensure winter preparedness and business continuity plans are in place.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, following the publication of the Utility Regulator’s SONI TSO Governance report, published on 30 August 2022, whether he has considered a referral to the Competition and Markets Authority in relation to grid connections.
answer_text: This question was asked in the previous mandate and to the previous Minister for the Economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline the reasons for the delays in providing nursery school provision in Ballyclare.
answer_text: Nursery School education in the Ballyclare area is currently provided by Ballyclare Nursery School and there is no current identified need to expand the level of provision.I was pleased to confirm that Ballyclare Nursery School would relocate to the former Tir na nOg Primary School. This project is being taken forward by the Education Authority (EA) as managing authority for the school and it is currently anticipated that the school could move to the new premises by September 2023.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the Education Authority has taken the decision to halt minor works programmes in primary schools in the South Antrim constituency.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has not taken the decision to halt minor works programmes in primary schools in the South Antrim constituency.  With competing demands for capital investment and a limited capital budget, both my Department and the EA have had to prioritise minor works schemes to ensure the budget is targeted to the highest priority schemes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what discussions he has had with the Utility Regulator in relation to the Energy Price Guarantee and how this will be rolled out in Northern Ireland to ensure that NI prices will not exceed those in the rest of the UK.
answer_text: The support announced by the Prime Minister will be a welcome relief for people facing increases in their bills.  The promise of support extends to Northern Ireland and I believe that the easiest and quickest way for NI consumers to receive this support is through UK-wide delivery.		I will continue to call for the UK Government to ensure that its support measures are delivered to consumers in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what engagement his Department has had with the new Energy Supply Taskforce and by what mechanisms the Department and Utility Regulator will interact with the I-SEM to discuss pricing, security and supply.
answer_text: To date my Department has had no direct contact with the Energy Supply Taskforce. My officials continue to work through established mechanisms with BEIS and energy industry partners including the Utility Regulator, regarding energy security of supply and afforadability in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, following the recent publication of the Utility Regulator’s Decision Paper, whether he will conduct an independent investigation into the role of the System Operator for Northern Ireland in the capacity auction process.
answer_text: The Single Electricity Market (SEM) Committee is responsible for the design, implementation and operation of the capacity auction process in the SEM.  The SEM Committee is the decision-making authority for all SEM matters and consists of three representatives from the Utility Regulator, the Commission for the Regulation of Utilities along with an independent and a deputy independent member. The Committee is currently carrying out a consultation seeking views on a review of the performance of the SEM Capacity Remuneration Mechanism (CRM) of which the capacity auctions form a part.  This review provides an opportunity for comment on the broad spectrum of issues relating to the CRM and seeks to ensure the auction process continues to operate effectively, delivering benefit for all consumers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what processes he has put in place to ensure the continuity and security of Electricity Supply in Northern Ireland, following the recent publication of the Utility Regulator’s Decision Paper.
answer_text: The Department continues with the existing processes in regard to ensuring continuity and security of supply. Officials regularly engage with the energy industry partners to ensure that electricity supply is maintained over the short, medium and long term. 		Officials are currently reviewing the industry’s winter preparedness and business continuity plans and engaging in contingency exercises ahead of the coming winter.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he continues to have confidence in the System Operator for Northern Ireland as a major strategic controller of infrastructure following the publishing of the Utility Regulator's Decision paper on modifications to the SONI Transmission System Operator licence.
answer_text: Whilst the Utility Regulator (UR) is an independent body, responsible for regulating the electricity industry in Northern Ireland, I greatly value the work carried out in regards to modifying the conditions of SONI's transmission system operator's license. It is vitally important that the interests of the Northern Ireland consumer are protected. The modifications are designed to mitigate risks identified by the UR, enhance the independence of SONI, improve the transparency and accountability of the company through better governance and management, and ultimately ensure the interests of Northern Ireland consumers are protected.It is essential that its governance and management structures command public confidence and are fit for purpose in dealing with the challenges ahead.I continue to support the work of the UR in promoting and protecting the interests of electricity consumers and I consider it essential that appropriate governance arrangements, consistent with legislative requirements and underpinned by licence conditions, are in place for SONI, ensuring that the interests of Northern Ireland consumers are paramount. The UR has taken steps to address the concerns highlighted in its report.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the cost on all NI consumers created by the poor governance and control mechanisms reported by the Utility Regulator on the System Operator for Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Utility Regulator (UR) is an independent body responsible for regulating the electricity industry in Northern Ireland and its work on the review of the System Operator for Northern Ireland’s (SONI) governance structures is in line with its statutory duties.		My Department is not in a position to provide an assessment of the cost to NI consumers, that is being addressed by the UR through its work on SONI governance. The UR has, however, advised me that the current SONI governance arrangements could potentially give rise to inappropriately higher prices for NI consumers.  Consequently, it is important that SONI is accountable to the UR and NI stakeholders, and that its decisions are transparent. The UR’s licence modification will provide greater transparency, including of cost information, together with an effective SONI Board to oversee and approve the SONI’s costs.		I continue to support the work of the UR in promoting and protecting the interests of electricity consumers. It is essential that appropriate governance arrangements, consistent with legislative requirements and underpinned by licence conditions are in place for SONI, ensuring that the interests of Northern Ireland consumers are paramount. The UR has taken steps to address concerns, including those in relation to cost, through the proposed licence modifications.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether has had any discussions with electricity generating & supply industry in Northern Ireland on the Integrated Single Electricity Market in relation to differential energy costs and regulatory priorities across the island.
answer_text: The Single Electricity Market (SEM) is the wholesale market on the island of Ireland where electricity generators and suppliers trade the power used by homes and businesses across the island. Through the Energy Trading Arrangements (ETA) within the SEM, generators compete to sell electricity. A number of markets each spanning different trading timeframes (Forward, Day Ahead, Intraday and the Balancing markets) have been designed within SEM to enable increasing levels of competition that place a downward pressure on prices whilst ensuring that the supply of power matches demand. The prices generated through the SEM apply equally to Northern Ireland and Ireland. There are no differential wholesale energy costs.The Single Electricity Market Committee (SEMC), which comprises of the Utility Regulator, the Irish Commission for Regulation of Utilities, and two independent members, regulates the Single Electricity Market (SEM). The policies and market rules decided on by the SEM Committee apply equally to Northern Ireland and Ireland.As such, I have had no discussions with the industry on the SEM in relation to differential energy costs or regulatory priorities across the island.I support the work of the Utility Regulator through the SEM Committee in promoting and protecting the interests of consumers in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 156/22-27, whether the decision to refuse planning permission for the Hightown incinerator remains extant; and whether his Department intends to reconsider this decision.
answer_text: The extant final decision notice refusing this application was issued by my Department on 1 April 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, further to reports that a senior official in his Department asked his predecessor to reverse her decision to refuse planning permission for the Hightown incinerator, for his Department’s current position on the decision not to approve planning permission for the Hightown incinerator; and whether the Department’s previous Permanent Secretary acted contrary to the Northern Ireland Civil Service Code in her representations to the Minister on learning of the Minister’s decision.
answer_text: The Member will be aware that my Department is in receipt of a pre action protocol letter in relation to its decision to refuse the planning application. As such further comment on the matter is not appropriate.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, further to reports that a senior official in his Department asked his predecessor to reverse her decision to refuse planning permission for the Hightown incinerator, whether he has investigated the (i) relationship; and (ii) communication between senior departmental officials, Arc21 and other companies supporting the construction of the proposed incinerator, before and after the Ministerial decision was taken in September 2015 to refuse the application.
answer_text: The Member will be aware that my Department is in receipt of a pre action protocol letter in relation to its decision to refuse the planning application. As such further comment on the matter is not appropriate.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) what assurances he has received from the Utility Regulator that the continuity and security of Northern Ireland’s electricity supply will be maintained for the next 12 months; and (ii) what safeguards he has sought from the electricity generators and the grid providers that Northern Ireland's electricity supply will not be threatened by planned decommissioning or projected maintenance.
answer_text: DfE and the Utility Regulator have regular meetings to review, monitor and if necessary take action in relation to current challenges to security of electricity supply, based on information provided from relevant electricity industry stakeholders.  SONI, the system operator, is required by licence to produce an annual Generation Capacity Statement which details the likely balance between electricity demand and supply. These exchanges will continue over the next 12 months. DfE officials work closely with SONI and NIE Networks who develop and implement outage plans.  The co-ordination of planned outages reduces the risk of disruption to NI electricity supply.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice why (i) there are discrepancies in the results of Occupational Health and Safety decisions on health retirements within the Prison Service; and (ii) there is no commonality of approach taken between grades of prison officer and those in managerial grades, in relation to retirement on health grounds.
answer_text: (i) The Occupational Health Service (OHS) have advised me that they are unaware of the alleged discrepancies. OHS Medical officers apply the relevant ill health retirement criteria of the member’s pension scheme. Their opinion is based on the medical evidence available and considers the likely long-term ability to carry out the duties of the post as identified by the Northern Ireland Prison Service. (ii) OHS Medical officers opinion are made using the criteria set out in the member’s pension scheme and medical evidence available and an assessment of the likely long term impact on an individual's ability to carry out the duties of their grade / post as identified by the NIPS. The same approach is adopted in all Northern Ireland Civil Service roles and grades, including the NIPS.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether it is the policy of her Department to continue with dismissal proceedings within the Prison Service, when affected prison officers have been given Occupational Health and Safety medical appeal dates that are after the dismissal date.
answer_text: Thank you for your question.  To ensure you receive a sufficient response I have requested further information from another department. Once this information is received, a full response will issue.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) what assessment her Department has made on prison officer morale; and (ii) whether she considers a detailed review of prison officer recruitment, retention and support is required.
answer_text: Ensuing staff were supported was a key focus of the ‘People’ strand of the Northern Ireland Prison Service’s (NIPS) continuous improvement programme ‘Prisons 2020’.  In addition to this, I initiated and published a report in January 2021 entitled ‘A Review of Support Services for Operational Prison Staff’, which has delivered a number of wellbeing initiatives including post critical incident support; a review of shift patterns; and increased staff engagement.  Like many front-line services, our prisons were greatly impacted by the pandemic with prison staff demonstrating resilience and professionalism in supporting the people in our care.  As the Service recovers from the pandemic, the well-being of staff will remain a priority as part of the next programme for continuous improvement ‘Prisons 25 by 25’. It will launch later this year and will build upon the progress made with ‘Prisons 2020’.  As part of my visits to prisons, I have met and discussed many issues of importance to prison officers.  I have always found them to be dedicated and motivated public servants.(ii) From September 2019 to date, NIPS has taken forward a number of external recruitment competitions, namely Custody Prison Officer in 2019 and 2021; Night Custody Officer in 2020 and 2022; and Prisoner Custody Officer (Driver /Escort) in 2020. These external recruitment opportunities were advertised through a range of media outlets including newspapers, radio and on-line. During this period a range of internal career progression opportunities have also been open to existing staff.NIPS continues to look for new and innovative ways to increase applications from as wide an applicant pool as possible and to ensure it has the right people, with the right skills, in the right posts, at the right time. To this end, as well as embracing the use of video interviewing as a result of the COVID-19 pandemic, the latest recruitment competition for Custody Prison Officers introduced bespoke prison-based situational judgement tests as part of the selection process. NIPS has also removed the requirement for GCSE qualifications for the most recent Night Custody Officer competition with candidates completing two online assessments which test key competencies required for the role.   In relation to retention figures (refer to Table 1 below) for prison officer grades, NIPS has a lower leaver rate than HM Prison and Probation Service (HMPPS) and continues to report a downward trend in the numbers of officers leaving the Service since 2018.Table 1: NIPS Leaver Rates 2018/19 to 2020/2021Uniformed Prison Staff NICSOverallHMPPS1 April 2018- 31March 2019             8.5%     3.5%    10.0%1 April 2019- 31 March 2020             8.1%     3.6%    10.5%1 April 2020- 31 March 2021             3.8%     2.8%    8.3%Notes: NIPS DataSource- HR Connect and additional DoJ DatabasesLeaver Rate Collection- (Number of Leavers/Staff in Post)*100Data is not yet available for the 2021/22 year

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether he has received a bid from the Minister for Infrastructure for additional financial support for NI Water, to mitigate rising energy costs.
answer_text: While the Department for Infrastructure has highlighted the pressures faced by NI Water and the wider Department as a result of rising energy costs, due to a lack of an Executive it has not been possible to agree a budget or consider formal bids.My Department’s Permanent Secretary has written to his Permanent Secretary colleagues asking them to provide returns detailing the pressures which their departments are facing, including energy costs.  This will help inform a budget process.I anticipate that the Department for Infrastructure’s response will include an updated position for NI Water’s energy cost pressures.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he has sought additional financial support for NI Water from the Minister of Finance to mitigate the rise in energy costs.
answer_text: NI Water, as the biggest consumer of energy here, is significantly impacted by the rise in energy costs, and is facing an inescapable resource pressure of £51m in 2022-23. Energy costs remain extremely volatile and therefore this pressure is subject to change.My Department continues to engage with the Department of Finance in relation to its financial position however the refusal of the DUP to form an Executive means £420m of funds held at the centre cannot be released and I cannot bid for in year funding. The blocking of the Executive also means a three year budget cannot be agreed which makes it extremely difficult for my Department to plan future investment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline the final outcome of the 21-22 in-year monitoring position, broken down by department.
answer_text: The 2021-22 Final Plan position for departments is provided within the Written Ministerial Statement to the Assembly 3 March 2022 and details Non ring-fenced Resource DEL and Capital Del including Financial Transactions Capital. The Statement can be accessed via the link below:Written Ministerial Statement 2021-22 Further 3 March 2022Table 1 provides the ring-fenced Resource DEL Final Plan position for departments.Provisional Outturn which details both final plan and provisional by department will be notified in due course and published on the DoF website.Table 1: 2021-22 Final Plan for NI Departments – Ring-fenced Resource DEL  Final Plan £ millionDAERA24.4DfC12.4DfE608.9DE1.2DoF34.9DoH171.9DfI119.0DOJ82.9TEO5.5FSA0.1NIA3.2NIAO0.1NIAUR0.0NIPSO0.1PPS1.8Total Departments1,066.3

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, further to media reports that £421m cannot currently be spent as a result of the absence of an Executive, can he provide a breakdown of (i) where this money has come from, broken down by department; and (ii) the additional £121m accumulated since March 2022.
answer_text: I can confirm the £421 million has arisen as a result of additional funding provided through the Chancellor's Spring Statement and Westminster Estimates process.  In March I indicated £300 million was available as a result of £100 million Treasury agreed carry forward, £150 million arising from Barnett consequentials due to the Energy Rebate scheme and a further £50 million held for carry forward by the Executive under the normal Budget Exchange Scheme.  Since then the Spring Statement provided a further £33.8 million of consequentials and the Main Estimate Process in Westminster has provided a further £87.6 million of Barnett consequentials.  This has resulted in the total available having risen to £421.4 million, which is some £121.4 million above the position I outlined earlier this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what action he is taking to address limitations of the data captured on taxes, as highlighted in the Fiscal Commission’s Final Report More fiscal devolution for Northern Ireland, May 2022.
answer_text: This is an issue I have been highlighting for some time now, so I very much welcome the Fiscal Commission recommending the need to improve data on tax receipts in the North. HMRC and ONS are primarily responsible for producing much of this information, which will be crucial in informing any future decisions regarding further devolution. My officials are now considering the report in detail and will engage directly with both ONS and HMRC as appropriate to consider how the quality of this data can be improved. More broadly, my officials in NISRA have been working over recent years to improve our economic data, and in particular the Economic Accounts, which will be important for the new independent forecasting arrangements the Commission recommended the Fiscal Council should put in place.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether senior civil servants working with the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs and found to have breached Northern Ireland Civil Service rules will be brought before a review or disciplinary panel.
answer_text: It is the responsibility of the Permanent Secretary to ensure that any breaches by DAERA staff of the general principles and rules of conduct covering all staff in the Northern Ireland Civil Service are dealt with in line with NICS disciplinary procedures.  I am advised that these procedures are specifically designed not only as a means of imposing disciplinary penalties but as a means of emphasising and encouraging improvements in an individual’s conduct.While the Department cannot comment on any individual cases, it is committed to following those procedures correctly and in a consistent and fair manner where there is a need to do so.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what plans he has to undertake an assessment of the cost of proposals in party manifestos to ascertain the impact of the proposals on the 2022-25 draft budget.
answer_text: It will be for the incoming Executive to agree a Budget based on its priorities.  Each Minister will provide assessments of their department’s requirements and the Executive will make collective decisions on a final budget.  It is not for my department to cost proposals made in party manifestos.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he has had any engagement with the Northern Ireland Utility Regulator regarding the governance structures within the System Operator for Northern Ireland; and what assurances he can give that consumers have not been overcharged for their electricity.
answer_text: The Utility Regulator (UR) is an independent body responsible for regulating the electricity industry in Northern Ireland and its work on the review of System Operator for Northern Ireland (SONI) governance is a matter for it in line with its statutory duties. However, during the course of this work, UR has provided my Department with updates on the progress of the review.  I support the work of the Regulator in promoting and protecting the interests of electricity consumers and I consider it essential that appropriate governance arrangements, consistent with legislative requirements and underpinned by licence conditions, are in place for SONI, ensuring that the interests of Northern Ireland consumers are paramount.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on (i) when the funding limits, as voted on account, will be reached; and (ii) the dates when a budget bill for 2022-2023 must be introduced.
answer_text: (i) Each department has an individual amount voted on account, and the rate at which each department will require to draw cash will differ; therefore the point at which the limit in the Vote on Account will be reached will differ from one department to another. It is anticipated this will begin to happen in late summer / early autumn.(ii) If a Budget Act is not in place at the time that one or more departments reach the limit of the Vote on Account, the Department of Finance Permanent Secretary can, from the 31 July 2022, approve the issue of cash under the power in Section 59 of the Northern Ireland Act 1998 and use of resources under the powers in Section 7 of the Government Resources and Accounts Act (Northern Ireland) 2001 up to 95% of the total amounts authorised for the Block as a whole for the 2021-22 financial year in the Budget Act (Northern Ireland) 2022. As these authorisations would be made on a department by department basis, and would be based on each individual department’s forecast expenditure, it is not possible to say with certainty exactly when the limit of these powers would be exhausted making the introduction of a Budget Bill essential, but it is anticipated that it would be towards the end of the 2022 calendar year. I would hope that a Budget Bill, based on a Budget agreed by the incoming Executive, will be introduced before that point is reached.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether her Department is aware of morale issues within the Northern Ireland Prison Service; and whether she will initiate a review into the process for managing requests from staff seeking retirement on the grounds of ill health.
answer_text: Ensuring staff are supported was a key focus of the ‘People’ strand of the Northern Ireland Prison Service’s continuous improvement programme ‘Prisons 2020’. In addition, I initiated and published a report in January 2021 entitled ‘A Review of Support Services for Operational Prison Staff’, which has delivered a number of wellbeing initiatives including the scheme to support staff post critical incident, a review of shift patterns, and increased staff engagement.Like many front-line services, our prisons was greatly impacted by the pandemic, with prison staff demonstrating resilience and professionalism in supporting the people in our care.  As the Service recovers from the pandemic, the staff will remain a priority as part of the programme for improvement ‘Prisons 25 by 25’. It launches this year and will further build upon the progress already made and foster a culture of excellence.  As part of my visits to prisons I have met and discussed many issues of importance to prison officers.  I have always found them to be dedicated and motivated public servants.The process for managing requests from staff seeking retirement on the grounds of ill health are outlined within the agreed NICS policies, applicable to all staff. All decisions taken on retirement on the grounds of ill health are based on medical advice provided by the Medical Officers in Occupational Health Service within the Department of Finance, following their confirmation the criteria for retirement on grounds of ill health has been met.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what additional resources can be provided to schools to support refugee children from Ukraine; and whether schools will be given flexibility regarding the numbers of children in year groups to accommodate children from Ukraine.
answer_text: The Education Authority’s Intercultural Education Service (IES) is providing advice to Ukrainian families and to schools on issues such as school admission, free school meals, school uniform grants, home-to-school transport and access to learning devices.  IES are working at the frontline in Ukrainian Arrival Centres, supported by my officials, and have set-up a specific webpage listing the supports available to schools who have registered Ukrainian pupils.  This includes provision of information about the Schools Trauma Advisory and Referral Service (STARS), trauma training and other emotional wellbeing advice.  Language and curriculum support is also available to schools via the IES online helpdesk.Schools will need to consider whether they can safely accommodate additional pupils while adhering to the current legislation and health and safety guidance on class sizes, including practical class sizes.  Where a school has no available places and wishes to admit children from Ukraine, my Department may provide approval for additional places at the school to permit their admission.My Department is represented on the Strategic Planning Group (SPG) for Asylum Seekers and Refugees which is chaired by The Executive Office and meets fortnightly to support those arriving from Ukraine.  My Department will bid for any additional funding made available for arrivals to cover educational costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether he has directed NICS HR to adopt a policy to refuse retirement on the grounds of ill health to members of the Prison Service.
answer_text: I have not made any such direction.  All decisions taken by NICSHR on retirement on the grounds of ill health are taken forward on the basis of medical advice provided by the Medical Officers in Occupational Health Service and following their confirmation the criteria for retirement on grounds of ill health has been met.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) whether he will uphold the decision of his predecessor in relation to the application for the ARC21 Hightown Incinerator; and (ii) whether he will direct his Department to implement his decision.
answer_text: A decision notice refusing this application was issued by my Department on 1 April 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update (i) on the relocation of Ballyclare Nursery School; and (ii) why the process has been delayed by the Education Authority.
answer_text: I can advise that officials from my Department met with senior officers from the Education Authority (EA) on 11 May to discuss the relocation of Ballyclare Nursery School.  A meeting has subsequently been arranged with the Principal and Chair of the Board of Governors for 15 June, at which the EA will be in attendance.  I am optimistic that this meeting will provide clarity on the outstanding issues, including the lease, draft programme of work and the availability of budget.I am not aware of this project having been delayed by the Education Authority, as work on the draft lease has been ongoing for many months. I understand that the terms of the lease are expected to be finalised within the next few months with the renovation work to follow in accordance with EA’s prioritised programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she has received correspondence from the Head of the Northern Ireland Prison Service in relation to changes to policy relating to managing requests from staff seeking retirement on the grounds of ill health.
answer_text: I have not received any correspondence from the Director General of the Northern Ireland Prison Service in relation to changes to policy relating to managing requests from staff seeking retirement on the grounds of ill health.NICSHR in collaboration with the Northern Ireland Prison Service manages sickness absence cases in accordance with the NICS Sickness Absence policies.  This may include a medical examination and consideration of early retirement and any self-referrals made by staff seeking to be considered for ill health retirement. Responsibility for the provision of medical advice to the Prison Service and indeed all NICS Departments sits with the Occupational Health Service within the Department of Finance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether (i) there has been a change in policy within the Prison Service with respect to the recognition of post-traumatic stress disorder as a disability; and (ii) the increase in prison officers being dismissed, and not eligible for disability pensions, is as a result of changes introduced by NICS HR.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Prison Service recognises all medical conditions including post-traumatic stress disorder. Staff who remain on long term sickness absence with no prospect of a return to work and who do not meet the established criteria for early medical retirement may be dismissed from the Service in line with the NICS Inefficiency Sickness Absence Policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether the Northern Ireland Civil Service HR has changed the time intervals provided for the recognition of disability within the Civil Service, in particular, the Northern Ireland Prison Service.
answer_text: It is not for the Northern Ireland Civil Service (NICS), Human Resources (HR) or the Northern Ireland Prison Service to make changes to the statutory time intervals for the recognition of disability.  It is not set by the NICS but rather HR is guided by the 1995 Disability Discrimination Act (NI).  The Act outlines that a disability must be a physical or mental impairment which has a substantial and long term adverse effect on a persons ability to carry out normal day to day activities.  Long term under the Act is defined as the effects have lasted at least 12 months.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he has authorised final planning approval for developments on the northern section of the Ballyclare Relief Road.
answer_text: I announced that consent should be granted for the development on 20th May 2022, and I anticipate that the final decision notice will issue shortly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether there are results of the investigation into the death of pets at Lough Neagh.
answer_text: I was sorry to hear about the recent deaths of young dogs in Rea’s Wood area of Lough Neagh. Officials in NIEA continue to liaise and work closely with colleagues in Antrim and Newtownabbey Borough Council (ANBC) to assist them with their investigation. NIEA officials have inspected the area for visual signs of water pollution. No blue green algae were found in water samples examined at the NIEA Water Management Unit in Lisburn.  A water sample was taken for a broad sweep of organic substances and nothing of significance was detected. The substances that were identified are commonly found at low levels in rivers and lakes across NI.  The results of all our analyses have been shared with ANBC to assist with their investigation.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of (i) prison officer staff, and grades, currently on sick leave due to post-traumatic stress disorder; (ii) prison officers under warning for dismissal; and (iii) staff within the Prison Service who are on extended sick leave for post-traumatic stress disorder as (a) a diagnose; or (b) part diagnose.
answer_text: All sickness absence within the Northern Ireland Prison Service and wider NICS is categorised under a number of general categories for reporting purposes.  Post-traumatic stress disorder is recorded as an illness under the broad range of mental health illnesses, namely ‘Anxiety/Stress/Depression/Other Psychiatric illnesses’.  Therefore, the information is not available in the format requested. As with any member of the NICS, Prison Officers who are on long term sickness are managed in line with the NICS Inefficiency Sickness Absence Policy.  This can include a consideration of dismissal.The NICS Sickness Absence policies defines sickness absence as either a frequent short term absence, that is where there has been four occasions or ten working days in a rolling 12 month period; or  long-term absence, where the absence has lasted for 20 consecutive working days or more.  There is no provision within the policies to extend any sickness absences.  With regard to the current number of staff on long-term sickness absence the information is also not available in the format requested, as clarified above.The primary aim in managing long-term sickness absence is to help facilitate a return to work at the earliest reasonable point.
